- Text: EXAMINATION:  CTA Torso

INDICATION:  History: ___ with SOB and tachycardia // eval for PE history of
pancreatic mass

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of intravenous contrast in the arterial
phase.  Then, imaging was obtained through the abdomen and pelvis in the
portal venous phase. Reformatted coronal and sagittal images through the
chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and oblique maximal intensity projection images of
the chest were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 2.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 7.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 3.8
mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 3.4 s, 26.6 cm; CTDIvol = 14.0 mGy (Body) DLP = 372.3
mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 5.2 s, 56.9 cm; CTDIvol = 14.0 mGy (Body) DLP = 795.1
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,171 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  Outside reference CT abdomen from ___
Outside reference CT chest from ___

FINDINGS: 

CHEST:

HEART AND VASCULATURE: There is a thrombus seen within the left main pulmonary
artery which is seen extending into the lingula and left lower lobe.  The
thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without evidence of dissection or
intramural hematoma.  No pericardial effusion is seen.  There is mild right
heart strain.  The main pulmonary artery is enlarged measuring up to 3.8 cm,
compatible with pulmonary artery hypertension.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM: No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar
lymphadenopathy is present.  No mediastinal mass.

PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS: There is again diffuse interstitial lung disease with traction
bronchiectasis and honeycombing seen in the upper lobes as well as in the
lower lobes bilaterally.  However, there are increased superimposed opacities
in the lung bases as well as in the upper lobes with regions of ground-glass
density and more confluent consolidation which are concerning for multifocal
infection.  A prominent cyst or pneumatocele is noted in the left lung base.

BASE OF NECK: Visualized portions orf the base of the neck show no
abnormality.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions.  There is no evidence of intrahepatic
or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder is within normal limits.

PANCREAS: Patient has a known pancreatic mass with enhancing lesion within the
body of the pancreas, measuring 1.7 x cm  with diffuse cystic dilatation of
the main pancreatic duct, not well delineated on the prior CT from ___ due to noncontrast imaging on the prior examination.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS:  The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: The kidneys are atrophic in appearance, left greater than right. 
Multiple cortically based subcentimeter hypodensities are seen scattered
throughout the left kidney, too small to fully characterize but likely
representing simple cysts.  A 7 mm angiomyolipoma is noted in the right kidney
(series 2b: Image 129).  There is no evidence of focal renal lesions or
hydronephrosis. There is no perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout.  Diverticulosis is
noted in the sigmoid colon without diverticulitis.  Otherwise, the remaining
colon and rectum are within normal limits.  The appendix is not visualized. 
There is no free intraperitoneal fluid or free air.

PELVIS:

The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no free
fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: There is a fibroid uterus.

LYMPH NODES: There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Moderate atherosclerotic
disease is noted.

BONES AND SOFT TISSUES: The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.
There is a a 7 x 3 cm peripherally enhancing fluid collection at the level of
the right greater trochanter (series 2b: Image 174 and series 607b: Image 42),
possibly representing bursal fluid however ultrasound-guided aspiration is
recommended to exclude infection.  There is levoconvex scoliosis of the lumbar
spine, centered at L3 level.  There is mild retrolisthesis of L4-L5 level. 
Advanced multilevel degenerative disc disease is seen throughout the
thoracolumbar spine.  There is a minimal retrolisthesis of L2 on L3-L3 on
L4-L4 L5 and L5 on S1.  Advanced facet degenerative changes are present within
the mid lower lumbar spine.

IMPRESSION:


1. There is a thrombus seen within the left main pulmonary artery which is
seen extending into the lingula left lower lobe.  There is mild right heart
strain with pulmonary artery hypertension.
2. known pancreatic mass along the pancreatic body with dilatation of the main
pancreatic duct for which further evaluation with MRI/MRCP may be performed.
3. Interstitial lung disease is again seen in the upper lobes and lower lobes
bilaterally with superimposed opacities which are concerning for multifocal
infection.
4. Ovoid fluid collection measuring 7 x 3.6 cm of abutting the right greater
trochanter for which percutaneous drainage may be performed to exclude
infection.  No CT evidence for osteomyelitis at this time.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  An MRI could be obtained to assess for additional smaller
lesions.

- Text: EXAMINATION:  CT ABD AND PELVIS W/O CONTRAST

INDICATION:  NO_PO contrast; History: ___ with LLQ abdominal pain, guaiac pos
NO_PO contrast  // ? diverticulitis

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector CT images of the abdomen and pelvis were acquired
without intravenous contrast. Non-contrast scan has several limitations in
detecting vascular and parenchymal organ abnormalities, including tumor
detection.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Spiral Acquisition 6.4 s, 50.6 cm; CTDIvol = 23.1 mGy (Body) DLP =
1,168.1 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,168 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT abdomen and pelvis from ___.  Mammography ___.

FINDINGS: 

LOWER CHEST: Consolidation at the lung bases could be sequeal of pulmonary
edema or pneumonia.  No pleural or pericardial effusion.  Stable severe
cardiac enlargement.  Aortic valve calcifications are severe.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates homogeneous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions within the limitations of an unenhanced
scan.  There is no evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary
dilatation.  The gallbladder is not visualized.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions within the limitations of an unenhanced scan.  There is no
pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: The kidneys are again noted to be mildly atrophic.  Bilateral simple
cyst appear unchanged.  Additional subcentimeter hypodensities are too small
to characterize by CT but likely represent cysts.  There is no suspicious
renal lesions within the limitations of an unenhanced scan.  There is no
hydronephrosis.  There is no nephrolithiasis. There is no perinephric
abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber and wall thickness throughout. Colonic diverticulosis without
pericolonic fat stranding or colonic wall thickening.

PELVIS: The urinary bladder is under distended which limits evaluation,
however appears unremarkable.  There is no free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: The visualized reproductive organs are unremarkable.
Small fundal fibroid is redemonstrated.

LYMPH NODES: There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Severe atherosclerotic
disease is noted.
Moderate dorsal spondylosis, unchanged.

BONES: There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture.

SOFT TISSUES: Small fat containing right inguinal hernia. Left breast mass
like lesion, better evaluated on prior mammography of ___.

IMPRESSION:


1. No acute process within the abdomen or pelvis to explain the patient's
pain, within the limitations of a non-enhanced scan.
2. Consolidations at the lower lobes bilaterally could represent pneumonia or
pulmonary edema.
3. Mass-like lesion in the left breast, better characterized by prior
mammography of ___.

NOTIFICATION:  The updated findings of lower lobe consolidations possibly
representing pneumonia or pulmonary edema were discussed with ___, M.D.
by ___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 10:26 am, 5
minutes after discovery of the findings.

HISTORY:  ___ woman with a NSCLC in mediastinum.  PET 
for staging. 
  
COMPARISON:  Comparison is made to chest CT ___ 
CT abdomen pelvis ___ 
  
TECHNIQUE:  ISOTOPE DATA: (___) 5.0 mCi ___ FDG; LAB DATA:

Glucose; CT DLP: 12 mGy-cm. 
Approximately 1 hour after intravenous administration of F-18 
fluorodeoxyglucose 
(FDG), non-contrast CT images were obtained for attenuation 
correction and for 
fusion with emission PET images. (The non-contrast CT images are 
not used to 
diagnose disease independently of the PET images.) A series of 
overlapping 
emission PET images was then obtained. The area imaged spanned 
the region from 
the skullbase to the proximal thighs. 
  
Computed tomography (CT) images were co-registered and fused 
with emission PET 
images to as sist with the anatomic localization of tracer 
uptake. The 
determination of the site of tracer uptake seen on PET data can 
have important 
implications regarding the significance of that uptake. 
  
FINDINGS:  HEAD/NECK: No abnormal FDG uptake in the head or 
neck.  Imaged 
thyroid gland is unremarkable.  No supraclavicular 
lymphadenopathy. 
  
CHEST: Again seen is enlarged subcarinal mediastinal mass 
extending into the 
right hilar area measuring approximately 4.7 x 4.3 cm with SUV 
max of 13.8. Mass 
again appears to exert mass effect on the adjacent esophagus.  A 
stent is seen 
running along the length of the proximal esophagus and appears 
patent except 
distally where there is some debris within it.  A small amount 
of air is seen 
apparently extraluminal to the stent at the left side of the 
upper esophagus in 
close proximity to the posterior aspect of the trachea (CT 43). 
The mediastinal 
mass also appears to encircle the bronchus intermedius which 
also contains a 
stent and appears patent.  No other FDG avid mediastinal 
lymphadenopathy or 
mass.  There is a small right pleural effusion, decreased from 
prior.  There has 
been interval resolution of a left-sided pleural effusion.  
There is moderate 
apical predominant centrilobular emphysema.  No FDG avid lung 
lesions are seen.  
Two 7 mm nodules in the right lower lobe do not appear FDG avid 
(CT 67, 62). T 
  
here is atelectasis in the right middle lobe and mild 
atelectasis in the right 
lower lobe.  Lungs are otherwise clear. 
  
ABDOMEN/PELVIS: No abnormal FDG uptake in the abdomen or pelvis. 
 A 16 mm right 
adrenal nodule displays no FDG avidity.  Calcifications in the 
pancreas are 
suggestive of chronic pancreatitis. 
  
MUSCULOSKELETAL: The no abnormal FDG uptake is seen in the 
bones. 
  
Physiologic uptake is seen in the brain, myocardium, salivary 
glands, GI and GU 
tracts, liver and spleen. 
  
IMPRESSION: 1. FDG avid mediastinal mass centered in the 
subcarinal region with 
extension into the right hilar region measuring up to 4.7 cm 
exerts mass effect 
on the adjacent esophagus and bronchus intermedius both of which 
contain stents 
and appear patent.  
  
2. Two 7 mm right lower lobe pulmonary nodules do not appear FDG 
avid.  
  
3. Small amount of air along the external portion of the 
esophageal stent in the 
upper aspect of the esophagus in close proximity of the trachea 
raises concern 
for possible tracheoesophageal fistula.  Consider further 
evaluation with 
bronchoscopy.  
  
4. No evidence of FDG avid disease in the abdomen or pelvis.  In 
particular, a 
17 mm right adrenal nodule does not appear FDG avid.  
  
5. Interval decrease in size of small right pleural effusion.  
Interval

Brief Hospital Course:
Summary:
___ with COPD initially admitted as a transfer from ___ after 
presenting there with syncope in the context of nausea and ~35 
lb weight loss over about four months. She was hospitalized for 
about 1 month, with course notable for newly diagnosed poorly 
differentiated likely lung adenocarcinoma,
esophageal stenting for mediastinal-esophageal fistula, severe 
malnutrition and SVT. She was discharged home on tube feeds with 
planned close Oncology follow up.

#Poorly Differentiated Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer 
She presented originally to ___ on ___ after a syncopal 
episode while seated. At that time, she reported dysphagia and 
30 lb unintentional weight loss over weeks along with an 8 month 
history of diffuse abdominal pain. CTA at ___ notable for 
large mediastinal mass. Was transferred to ___ on ___. On 
___ she underwent am EBUS with pathology which showed highly 
atypical epithelial cells suspicious for Non-small cell 
carcinoma. On ___ she had an MRI which showed no intracranial 
metastatic disease. On ___ she had a PET scan which was
remarkable for a 4.7 cm right hilar mass without evidence of 
other FDG avid lesions. On ___, tumor board met and decided 
that the patient should have carboplatin/pemetrexed to 
cytoreduce before chemoradiation. She was given 
carboplatin/pemetrexed on ___, with plan to add pembrolizumab 
with next cycle (pathology from ___ with PDL1 expression 50%). 
She was started on vitamin B12 1000 mcg IM q9weekds and daily 
folate on ___ due to antimetabolite effects of pemetrexed.

#Severe Protein Calorie Malnutrition 
#Mediastinal-Esophageal Fistula
Severely malnourished in the setting of nausea, poor PO and 
extrinsic compression of mass on esophagus. On ___, she had a 
barium swallow which showed no TE fistula. On ___, she had an 
esophageal stent placed by GI. As she was felt to have 
esophageal collapse distal to stent, pain from stent and little 
to no improvement in PO intake, the stent was removed on ___. 
PEG was placed on that date. When stent removed, fistula between 
tumor and esophagus via mediastinum was noted. Stent was 
replaced on ___. Tube feeds were started on ___ through the 
PEG. We monitored her closely for refeeding syndrome throughout 
the hospitalization. She was cleared to have a pureed diet. Her 
tube feeds were transitioned to bolus feeding which was 
tolerated well. 

#Abdominal Pain 
#Nausea 
No clear anatomic source of abdominal pain was obvious on 
imaging. Felt to possibly be referred pain, chronic pancreatitis 
and/or electrolyte disturbances. For her abdominal pain and 
nausea, she had been treated with MS ___ 90 mg BID, morphine 
Sulfate 5 mg IV q2hrs PRN, Tylenol ___ q8hrs PRN and Zofran 4 mg 
PO q8hrs. As MS contin can't be crushed, this was changed to 
standing morphine 20 mg q4h on ___, with morphine 20 mg PO q2h 
PRN. Fentanyl patch 25 mcg was added ___.

#Supraventricular Tachycardia 
#Atrial Fibrillation 
In the ___, she had several episodes of SVT in the 200s. 
This was thought to be the cause of her syncope. Tele showed 
afib. Received IV metoprolol for this. SVT recurred on ___, and 
she was started on PO metop 25 BID which was uptitrated to 25 PO 
q6h. Diltiazem induced hypotension. After stent re-placement on 
___, she experienced intermittent afib in the PACU with rates 
up to 140s. She received IV metop x3 and her scheduled PO 25 mg. 
She was transferred to the FICU, where she did not require any 
additional IV metop. Her PO was uptitrated to 50 q6h, and she 
eventually transitioned to metoprolol succinate 200 mg for 
adequate rate control. Succinate could not be crushed; dose was 
changed to metoprolol tartrate 100 mg BID after discussion with 
Pharmacy. Given her very low CHADS2VASC2 score with a <0.6% risk 
of stroke/year, and risk of bleeding with fistula and tumor, we 
deferred anticoagulation at this time. 

#Post-Obstructive Pneumonia
#Hospital Acquired Pneumonia
Patient had 2 separate pneumonias during this hospitalization. 
She was admitted with and treated for a post obstructive PNA 
with 7d course of Unasyn through ___. On ___ developed fever 
and possible new R basal opacity on CXR, started on 
vanco/cefepime/flagyl. Narrowed to ceftriaxone for 7 day HAP/low 
risk MDR course. Sputum culture grew Moraxella.

#COPD
#Chronic Respiratory Failure 
Treated with PRN albuterol nebs, Duonebs and Acetylcysteine 20% 
nebs during admission. Due to patient preference for neb over 
inhaler, home nebulizer was arranged. Daily tiotropium added. 
Required ___ O2 via NC during admission, and was set up with 
home oxygen.

#Elevated bicarbonate

acidosis as well as contraction alkalosis. Followed by Renal

Mr. ___ was admitted to Dr. ___ service after 
bipolar transurethral resection of prostate. No concerning 
intraoperative events occurred; please see dictated operative 
note for details. He patient received ___ antibiotic 
prophylaxis. The patient's postoperative course was 
uncomplicated. He received intravenous antibiotics and 
continuous bladder irrigation overnight. On POD1 the CBI was 
discontinued. His urine was pink, clear and and without clots. 
He remained a-febrile throughout his hospital stay.  At 
discharge, the patient had pain well controlled with oral pain 
medications, was tolerating regular diet, ambulating without 
assistance, and versed on foley catheter care.  He was given 
pyridium and oral pain medications on discharge and a course of 
antibiotics along with explicit instructions to follow up in 
clinic.

Cosopt, Flomax, Bisoprolol-HCTZ

Home

Mental Status: Clear and coherent.

Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
-Do not lift anything heavier than a phone book (10 pounds) or 
drive until you are seen by your Urologist in follow-up

-You may continue to periodically see small amounts of blood in 
your urine--this is normal and will gradually improve

-Resume all of your pre-admission medications, except HOLD 
aspirin until you see your urologist in followup AND your foley 
has been removed (if not already done)

-Continue taking PROSCAR (Finasteride) AND/OR your other 
prostate shrinking medications until you are otherwise advised 
.

-You MAY be discharged home with a medication called PYRIDIUM 
that will help with the "burning" pain you may experience when 
voiding. This medication may turn your urine bright orange.

-Colace has been prescribed to avoid post surgical constipation 
and constipation related to narcotic pain medication. 
Discontinue if loose stool or diarrhea develops. Colace is a 
stool softener, NOT a laxative, and available over the counter.  
The generic name is DOCUSATE SODIUM.  It is recommended that you 
use this medication.

-Do not eat constipating foods for ___ weeks, drink plenty of 
fluids to keep hydrated

-No vigorous physical activity or sports for 4 weeks and while 
Foley catheter is in place. 

-Tylenol should be your first line pain medication, a narcotic 
pain medication has been prescribed for breakthrough pain >4. 
Replace Tylenol with narcotic pain medication.

-Max daily Tylenol (acetaminophen) dose is 4 grams from ALL 
sources, note that narcotic pain medication also contains 
Tylenol

-Do not drive or drink alcohol while taking narcotics and do not 
operate dangerous machinery. Also, if the Foley catheter and Leg 
Bag are in place--Do NOT drive (you may be a passenger).

IF YOU ARE DISCHARGED HOME WITH A FOLEY CATHETER:

-Please refer to the provided nursing instructions and handout 
on Foley catheter care, waste elimination and leg bag usage.

-Your Foley should be secured to the catheter secure on your 
thigh at ALL times until your follow up with the surgeon.

-Follow up in 1 week for wound check and Foley removal. DO NOT 
have anyone else other than your Surgeon remove your Foley for 
any reason.

-Wear Large Foley bag for majority of time, leg bag is only for 
short-term when leaving house.

-Start prescribed antibiotic (Ciprofloxacin) 1 day prior to 
scheduled Foley catheter removal and for two subsequent days

___

Brief Hospital Course:
Pt was admitted for elective transphenoidal resection of a 
nonfunctioning pituitary adenoma.  ENT assisted in the 
transnasal approach and a nasal trumpet and nasal packing were 
placed.  A lumbar drain was also placed to prevent CSF leak due 
to the quality of the dura at closing. Postoperatively she was 
extubated and transfered to the PACU.  She did well and remained 
neurologically intact.  Urine output, Urine chemistries and 
Serum Sodium were monitored closely for signs of DI.  
Endocrinology was consulted. The lumbar drain had a goal 
drainage of 30ml Q2hrs and she was kept on bedrest with bathroom 
privledges. On ___ she was neurologically intact and the lumbar 
drain was functioning well. All labs remained WNL. On ___ the 
hydrocortisone was discontinued and she was started back on her 
home dose of prednisone. In the evening her lumbar drain was 
clamped. 
On ___ the patient was again stable and did not have any sign of 
drainage from her nose. The lumbar drain was removed without 
complication. She was seen by physical therapy who recommended 
discharge to acute rehab.
On ___ she had stable labs and exam. She was cleared for 
discharge to home by physicial therapy.

prednisone 17mg, omeprazole, colace, vit d, tylenol, nifedipine, 
asa, calcium with vit D

Serum Na

Home With Service
 
Facility:
___
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Pituitary Adenoma

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Take your pain medicine as prescribed.
Exercise should be limited to walking; no lifting, straining, 
or excessive bending.
Increase your intake of fluids and fiber, as narcotic pain 
medicine can cause constipation. We generally recommend taking 
an over the counter stool softener, such as Docusate (Colace) 
while taking narcotic pain medication.
Unless directed by your doctor, do not take any 
anti-inflammatory medicines such as Motrin, Aspirin, Advil, and 
Ibuprofen etc. 
Clearance to drive and return to work will be addressed at 
your post-operative office visit.
Continue Sinus Precautions for an additional two weeks.  This 
means, no use of straws, forceful blowing of your nose, or use 
of your incentive spirometer. 
If you have been discharged on Prednisone, take it daily as 
prescribed.  If on any day, you are ill, take the prednisone as 
you have been instructed by the endocrine team. 
If you are required to take Prednisone, an oral steroid, make 
sure you are taking a medication to protect your stomach 
(Prilosec, Protonix, or Pepcid), as this medication can cause 
stomach irritation.  Prednisone should also be taken with a 
glass of milk or with a meal. 

CALL YOUR DOCTOR IMMEDIATELY IF YOU EXPERIENCE ANY OF THE 
FOLLOWING

New onset of tremors or seizures.
Any confusion or change in mental status. 
Any numbness, tingling, weakness in your extremities.
Pain or headache that is continually increasing, or not 
relieved by pain medication.
Any signs of infection at the wound site: redness, swelling, 
tenderness, or drainage.
It is normal for feel nasal fullness for a few days after 
surgery, but if you begin to experience drainage or salty taste 
at the back of your throat, that resembles a dripping 
sensation, or persistent, clear fluid that drains from your nose 
that was not present when you were sent home, please call. 
Fever greater than or equal to 101° F.
If you notice your urine output to be increasing, and/or 
excessive, and you are unable to quench your thirst, please call 
your endocrinologist.

___

- Text: INDICATION:  History of pituitary adenoma, presents with severe headache.

Coronal and sagittal reformations were provided.

FINDINGS:  The patient is status post transsphenoidal hypophysectomy with
stable crescentic low-density material noted in the posterior aspect of the
resection bed, likely representing fat packing.  No evidence of recurrent
mass.

There is no evidence of hemorrhage, edema, large masses, mass effect, or
infarction.  Mild mucosal thickening is evident within the sphenoid septum. 
The mastoid air cells, middle ear cavities and ethmoid air cells are clear. 
No soft tissue swelling identified.

IMPRESSION:  Post-surgical appearance of transsphenoidal hypophysectomy
without evidence of recurrence.  Mucosal thickening evident within the
sphenoid sinus.  No intracranial hemorrhage.  Prominent sulci and ventricles
consistent with age-related parenchymal involution.

There is no intra-axial or extra-axial hemorrhage, edema, shift of normally
midline structures, or evidence of acute major vascular territorial
infarction.  Periventricular and subcortical white matter hypodensities are
nonspecific, but likely reflect sequelae of chronic small vessel ischemic
disease.  Prominence of the ventricles and sulci suggest involutional changes.
Chronic bilateral basal ganglia small infarcts are again noted. 
Atherosclerotic vascular calcifications are noted of bilateral cavernous
portions of internal carotid arteries.    Mastoid air cells and middle ear
cavities are well aerated. The bony calvarium is intact.  Bilateral ethmoid
air cell and left maxillary sinus mucosal thickening is present.  An
approximately 4 mm probable pineal cyst is again noted.

IMPRESSION:


1.  Study is mildly degraded by motion.
2. No acute intracranial abnormality.  Please note MRI of the brain is more
sensitive for the detection of acute infarct.
3. No evidence acute intracranial hemorrhage or fracture.
4. Atrophy, probable small vessel ischemic changes, and atherosclerotic
vascular disease as described.
5. Paranasal sinus disease, as described.

- Text: EXAMINATION:   CT abdomen and pelvis

INDICATION:  NO_PO contrast; History: ___ with history of cholangiocarcinoma
with fever and elevated LFTs NO_PO contrast// eval or CBD stent and biliary
tree

TECHNIQUE:   Single phase split bolus contrast: MDCT axial images were
acquired through the abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast
administration with split bolus technique.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 5.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 26.5 mGy (Body) DLP =
13.2 mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 6.6 s, 51.6 cm; CTDIvol = 19.6 mGy (Body) DLP =
1,011.0 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 1,024 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  MRCP ___.
CT abdomen and pelvis ___

FINDINGS: 

LOWER CHEST: Consolidation in the right posterior lung base compatible with
chronic rounded atelectasis.  Pleural thickening and pleural plaques likely
reflect prior asbestos exposure.  There is no evidence of pleural or
pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions. The gallbladder is surgically absent.
There is mild peripheral and moderate central intrahepatic bile duct dilation
more marked on the left, similar to the prior MRCP from ___.  There
is mild pneumobilia compatible with biliary stenting and internal external
biliary drain.  A percutaneous internal to external biliary drain terminates
in the third part of the duodenum.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation.  There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
A 2.0 cm bilobed cyst or 2 adjacent cysts again noted in the inferior pole of
the left kidney.  There is no evidence of focal renal lesions or
hydronephrosis.  There is no perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout.  Diverticulosis of
the sigmoid colon is noted, without evidence of wall thickening and fat
stranding.  The appendix is normal.  Mild mesenteric fat stranding (misty
mesenteric appearance) is again noted.

PELVIS: The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: The visualized reproductive organs are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES: There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Mild atherosclerotic disease
is noted.

BONES: There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture. 
Severe multilevel degenerative changes are again noted.

SOFT TISSUES: A left inguinal hernia containing fat is noted with minimal
tethering of the bladder wall towards the hernia without frank bladder
herniation.  Severe multilevel degenerative changes are noted.

IMPRESSION: 

Internal-external biliary drain in addition to common bile duct stent. 
Biliary drain terminates in the third portion the duodenum. Mild to moderate
intrahepatic biliary ductal dilation (similar to previous MRCP) and minimal
pneumobilia.

Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ was admitted to the hospital following a right 
colectomy and hepatic wedge resection. For full details on this 
procedure, please refer to the operative note. She tolerated the 
procedure well and after stay in the PACU was transferred to the 
floor.

Ms. ___ pain was very well controlled with IV pain 
medications as well as an epidural. Her postoperative diet was 
clear liquids. She tolerated this without issue though 
postoperative day 1. Her home medications were resumed 
accordingly.

On postoperative day 2, her diet was advanced to regular. When 
she tolerated this her pain regimen was transitioned to oral 
medications, her epidural was capped and removed. Her foley was 
removed as well and she voided without issue. Her surgically 
placed ___ drain was removed and a stitch was placed in her 
skin to prevent leakage of peritoneal fluid from the site. Late 
in the day, after a large meal, she had four small episodes of 
emesis. Her nausea was controlled with Zofran and her emesis 
resolved with backing her diet down to clear liquids.

On postoperative day 3, her diet was advanced again to regular. 
She tried small, frequency meals with no further emesis or 
nausea. She was able to ambulated independently. Her pain was 
well controlled with oral medications alone. The decision was 
then made to discharge the patient home with outpatient follow 
up to be scheduled for next week.

Discharge Disposition:

Discharge Diagnosis:
Metastatic colon cancer

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Ms. ___, 

You were admitted to the hospital following resection of the 
right side of your colon as well as a resection of a piece of 
your liver. You have recovered well and it is time for your 
discharge home. Please refer to the following instructions:

From the liver team:
Please call Dr. ___ office at ___ for fever > 101, 
chills, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, increased 
abdominal pain, pain not controlled by your pain medication, 
swelling of the abdomen or ankles, worsening yellowing of the 
skin or eyes, itching, inability to tolerate food, fluids or 
medications, or any other concerning symptoms.
.
No lifting more than 10 pounds
.
No driving if taking narcotic pain medication
.
Drain and record the bile drain output twice daily and as needed 
so that the drain is never more than ½ full. Call the office if 
the drain output increases by more than 100 cc from the previous 
day, becomes bloody or develops a foul odor.
.
Change the drain dressing once daily or after your shower. Do 
not allow the drain to hang freely at any time. Inspect the site 
for redness, drainage or bleeding. Make sure there is a stitch 
at the drain site and the stat lock in place.

From the colon surgery team:
You have recovered from this procedure well and you are now 
ready to return home. 
Please monitor your bowel function closely. You may or may not 
have had a bowel movement prior to discharge which is 
acceptable; however it is important that you have a bowel 
movement in the next ___ days. After anesthesia it is not 
uncommon for patients to have some decrease in bowel function 
but you should not have prolonged constipation. However, you may 
have loose stool and passing of small amounts of dark, old 
appearing blood. If you notice that you are passing bright red 
blood with bowel movements or having large amounts of loose 
stool without improvement please call the office or go to the 
emergency room. While taking narcotic pain medications you are 
at risk for constipation, please take an over the counter stool 
softener such as Colace.
You have a long vertical surgical incisions on your abdomen. It 
is important that you monitor these areas for signs and symptoms 
of infection including: increasing redness of the incision 
lines, white/green/yellow/malodorous drainage, increased pain at 
the incision, increased warmth of the skin at the incision, or 
swelling of the area.
You may shower; pat the incisions dry with a towel, do not rub.  
If you have steri-strips (the small white strips), they will 
fall off over time, please do not remove them. Please do not 
take a bath or swim until cleared by the surgical team.
Pain is expected after surgery, this will gradually improve over 
the first week or so you are home.  You should continue to take 
2 Extra Strength Tylenol (___) for pain every 8 hours around 
the clock. Please do not take more than 3000mg of Tylenol in 24 
hours or any other medications that contain Tylenol such as cold 
medication. Do not drink alcohol while taking Tylenol.  You may 
also take Advil (Ibuprofen) 600mg every 8 hours for 7 days, 
please take Advil with food. If these medications are not 
controlling your pain to a point where you can ambulate and 
perform minor tasks, you should take a dose of the narcotic pain 
medication oxycodone.  Please do not take sedating medications, 
drink alcohol, or drive while taking the narcotic pain 
medication.
You may feel weak or "washed out" for up to 6 weeks after 
surgery. Do not lift greater than a gallon of milk for 3 weeks. 
At your post op appointment, your surgical team will clear you 
for heavier exercise.  In the meantime, you may climb stairs, 
and go outside and walk.  Please avoid traveling long distances 
until you speak with your surgical team at your post-op visit.
Thank you for allowing us to participate in your care, we wish 
you all the best!

CHEST:
The ETT terminates approximately 2.7 cm above the carina.

HEART AND VASCULATURE:  Dense atherosclerotic calcifications at the aortic
arch, the origin of the head and neck vessels, as well as the coronary
arteries.  Dense calcifications of the aortic valve.  The thoracic aorta is
normal in caliber without evidence of acute injury.  Moderate cardiac
enlargement is noted.  Otherwise, the heart, pericardium, and great vessels
are within normal limits. No pericardial effusion is seen.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM:  Numerous prominent mediastinal and left hilar
lymph nodes measuring up to 9 mm in short axis (series 2, image 16, 18) may be
reactive in nature.  No axillary lymphadenopathy.  No mediastinal mass or
hematoma.

PLEURAL SPACES:  There are small bilateral pleural effusions, left greater
than right.  No pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS:  There is near complete occlusion of the bilateral lower lobe
bronchi.  There are dense bilateral lower lobe consolidations, which raise
concern for aspiration or multifocal pneumonia.  However, some of these
opacities appear nodular (series 2, image 14, 25, 29), and an underlying
malignancy cannot entirely be excluded.

BASE OF NECK:  The thyroid is heterogeneous with multiple small calcified and
noncalcified nodules measuring up to 8 mm, none of which require follow-up in
a patient of this age per ACR guidelines.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: A 1.9 x 1.8 cm hypodensity within the right lobe of the liver
(series 2, image 45) is stable since at least ___, likely benign, possibly a
hemangioma.  Otherwise, the liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation
throughout.  There is no evidence of focal lesion or laceration.  There is no
evidence of intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder
is surgically absent.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesion or laceration.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: Multiple hypodensities within the kidneys bilaterally, some of which
are consistent with simple cysts, others which are too small to characterize,
but also likely represent simple cysts.  Otherwise, the kidneys are of normal
and symmetric size with normal nephrogram.  There is no evidence of enhancing
renal lesions or hydronephrosis.  There is no perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: There is large hiatal hernia.  The enteric tube terminates
in the hiatal hernia.  Small bowel loops demonstrate normal caliber, wall
thickness, and enhancement throughout.  The colon and rectum are within normal
limits.  The appendix is not visualized.  There is no evidence of mesenteric
injury.

There is no free fluid or free air in the abdomen.

PELVIS:  The bladder is decompressed by Foley, and cannot be adequately
evaluated on this examination.  There is no free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: The reproductive organs are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES: Enlarged portacaval lymph node measures approximately 1.5 x 1.4
cm (series 2, image 51), unchanged.  There are multiple prominent periaortic
retroperitoneal lymph nodes measuring up to 8 mm in short axis (series 2,
image 56).  A right common iliac lymph node measures up to 9 mm in short axis,
which is new (series 2, image 76).

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm or retroperitoneal hematoma. 
Extensive atherosclerotic disease is noted.  A right femoral venous catheter
is partially visualized.

BONES: The patient is status post right shoulder arthroplasty without evidence
of hardware complication. There is no acute fracture.  No focal suspicious
osseous abnormality.  Extensive degenerative changes with grade 1
anterolisthesis of L4 on L5.

SOFT TISSUES: The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

IMPRESSION:


1. Complete occlusion of the bilateral lower lobe bronchi with dense lower
lobe consolidations, which likely represents aspiration or multifocal
infection.  However, there are nodular opacities within the lower lobes
bilaterally, which could also be due to infection though followup will be
necessary to exclude the possibility of an underlying malignancy.
2. Small bilateral pleural effusions.
3. Large hiatal hernia.  The NG tube terminates in the hiatal hernia.
4. Multi nodular thyroid.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  Follow-up chest CT to ensure resolution of the nodular
opacities.

Brief Hospital Course:
Outpatient Providers: PATIENT SUMMARY
=================
Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with ___ primary 
progressive multiple sclerosis who presented after a few days of 
dysuria, urinary retention, fevers, and cough, admitted for flu 
treatment and urinary tract infection. Her hospital course was 
complicated by hypernatremia, hypokalemia, and aggressive 
behavior with refusal of care. 

ACTIVE ISSUES:
================
#UTI: Patient presented with fevers, abdominal pain, and 
dysuria. UA with moderate leukocytes and few bacteria but urine 
culture with fecal contamination. History of recurrent UTIs but 
have been mostly pan-sensitive bacterial infections on review of 
chart. Given pt's clear history of dysuria, despite negative 
culture, patient was treated with 3 days of ceftriaxone and 
discharged on 4 days of Macrobid PO 100 mg BID for total 7 day 
course of antibiotics. Patient had urinary retention while 
inpatient and receive straight catherization for PVR>600.  
However, on discharge, pt. provided history of urinating every 
___ hours at home which suggests chronic overflow incontinence, 
likely due to her MS.

#Influenza: Patient was found to be FluB positive with fevers 
and cough and body
aches. She was started on Tamiflu for 5 day course. 

#Toxic metabolic encephalopathy
#Aggressive behavior: the patient had toxic metabolic 
encephalopathy which manifested in verbal and physical 
aggressive behavior during first day of admission. The patient 
was difficult to reason and demanded to leave AMA despite 
life-threatening electrolyte abnormalities and multiple 
infections requiring treatment. She was determined not to have 
capacity to leave and refuse treatment as corroborated by 
psychiatric evaluation. Patient's mental status improved by the 
___ day of hospitalization and patient did not appear to have 
memory of these events. It is unclear whether her acute 
confusional state was related to her infection vs. toxic 
ingestion. Of note, her urine toxicology screen was amphetamine+ 
without clear explanation of false positive that could be 
explained by her home medications that we could PAML. 

CHRONIC ISSUES
================
#Multiple Sclerosis: patient reported that her MS symptoms are 
the same as her chronic symptoms. Her neurological exam 
performed by ED consultation of neurology team remained 
unchanged compared to her outpatient provider's. Patient 
continued on gabapentin, lamotrigine, clonazepam, and baclofen.

#GERD: continued on ranitidine

#HLD: continued on simvastatin

#Chronic pain: continued fentanyl patch, gabapentin, 
lamotrigine, clonazepam,
baclofen, tramadol as needed, and ibuprofen.

#Hypothyroidism: continued 12.5 mcg PO daily.

#CODE: Full (presumed)
#CONTACT: ___ ___ changed to her home health 
aide ___ 
___: caregiver 
___ number: ___ 

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES
==================
[] Hypokalemia/hypernatremia: Check BMP in ___ days to check for 
electrolyte abnormalities
[] Complicated cystitis: Pt being discharged on 4 more days of 
Macrobid for total of 7 days
[] Urinary retention: Pt had urinary retention with PVR of >700. 
Would perform straight catherization as needed and evaluate for 
overflow incontinence.
[] Urine tox+: Pt found to have amphetamine+ in urine without 
clear explanation of false positive that could be explained by 
her home medications that we could PAML. We recommend continuing 
to screen for this if patient is on a narcotic contract. It is 
unclear whether or not this was contributing to her aggressive 
behavior while inpatient.

- Text: INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with hx of MS, recurrent UTI, pw abdominal pain
and diarrhea following abx course, abdomen distended, TTP, concern for ileus
vs obstruction// evaluate for evidence of ileus, obstruction, colitis

TECHNIQUE:  Single phase split bolus contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired
through the abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast administration
with split bolus technique.
Oral contrast was administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 4.0 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 19.3 mGy (Body) DLP =
9.6 mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 6.0 s, 47.6 cm; CTDIvol = 8.8 mGy (Body) DLP = 416.4
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 426 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  ___ CT torso.

FINDINGS: 

LOWER CHEST: There is mild atelectasis of the dependent lung bases
bilaterally..  There is no evidence of pleural or pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: Scattered subcentimeter hypodensities within the right and left
hepatic lobes are unchanged from prior, too small to characterize but likely
represent hepatic cysts or biliary hamartomas.  The gallbladder is
unremarkable.  There is new mild intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation noted
bilaterally.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
There is no evidence of focal renal lesions or hydronephrosis.  There is no
perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout.  Fluid-filled
ascending and descending colonic bowel loops demonstrating marked wall
thickening with mucosal enhancement is consistent with patient's reported
history of diarrhea, most likely secondary to infectious colitis given its
distribution.  The transverse colon is spared appears more normal in caliber
without significant mural thickening or enhancement.  The appendix is normal.

PELVIS: The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: The uterus and bilateral adnexa are unremarkable.

LYMPH NODES: There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Moderate atherosclerotic
disease is noted.

BONES: There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture.

SOFT TISSUES: The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

IMPRESSION: 

Mural thickening and mucosal enhancement involving the ascending and
descending colon with sparing of the transverse colon, in the setting
air-fluid levels, is consistent with colitis.  Most likely infectious etiology
given its distribution.  Ischemic colitis is less likely but remains in the
differential.

NOTIFICATION:  The findings were discussed with ___, M.D. by ___,
M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 8:48 am, 1 minutes after discovery of
the findings.

## Radiology Examinations [2199-04-16 10:10:00]
- Note_Type: RR
- Exam_Name: ['CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST']
- Text: EXAMINATION:  CT abdomen pelvis with contrast.

INDICATION:  NO_PO contrast; History: ___ with fever, diarrhea, ESRD on
HDNO_PO contrast// assess for enteritis, abscess

TECHNIQUE:  Single phase split bolus contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired
through the abdomen and pelvis following intravenous contrast administration
with split bolus technique.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 6.0 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 28.9 mGy (Body) DLP =
14.4 mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 5.9 s, 46.6 cm; CTDIvol = 8.9 mGy (Body) DLP = 415.5
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 430 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  CT ___.

FINDINGS: 

LOWER CHEST: Small bibasilar atelectasis is demonstrated.  No pleural
effusion.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions.  The gallbladder is within normal
limits.  Prominence of the common bile duct measuring up to 1.2 cm is again
demonstrated, which appears similar to prior.  There is a 0.9 cm cystic mass
in the pancreatic head which appears to communicate with the main common bile
duct (series 2, image 27), which is unchanged in size from ___.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: The native kidneys are atrophic bilaterally, with a simple cyst
demonstrate off of the left renal cortex as well as hypodensities too small to
characterize by CT.  There is a renal transplant in the right lower quadrant. 
No hydronephrosis is demonstrated.  A intermediate density cyst in the
transplant kidney measuring up to 2.2 cm is unchanged from prior.  No
perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: The stomach is unremarkable.  Small bowel loops demonstrate
normal caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout.  The colon and
rectum are within normal limits.  The appendix is normal.

PELVIS: The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no
free fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: Calcifications are again demonstrated along the periphery
of the uterus, presumably due to fibroids.  There is a new calcification which
appears to be in the endometrium (series 602, image 37).

LYMPH NODES: There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy.  There
is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Moderate atherosclerotic
disease is noted.  A right groin central venous catheter terminates in the
right atrium.

BONES: There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or acute fracture.

SOFT TISSUES/RETROPERITONEUM: Thickening of the perirenal fascia on the right
is likely sequelae of prior right psoas abscess and is unchanged.  The left
psoas muscle is enlarged with a 2.0 x 1.2 cm region with what appears to be a
hematocrit level (series 2, image 44) and relative hypodensity along the
nondependent portion concerning for intramuscular psoas hematoma, no rim
enhancement is identified.

Along the tunneled tract of the right groin central line, there is prominence
of the canal with possible fluid along its course.

IMPRESSION:


1. No acute gastrointestinal process to correlate with the patient's symptoms.
2. New left psoas muscle thickening with concern for hematocrit level, psoas
hematoma.  No rim enhancement is identified.
3. Persistent common biliary dilatation to 1.2 cm.  No significant change in a
0.9 cm cystic pancreatic head lesion which appears to communicate with the
main common bile duct compared to ___.
4. New calcification appears to be within the endometrium, recommend
outpatient follow-up with pelvic sonography.
5. Along the tunneled tract of the right groin central line, there is
prominence of the canal with fluid seen tracking along the course of the
catheter, infection not excluded.  Recommend correlation with physical exam.

RECOMMENDATION(S):  Follow-up pelvic ultrasound for new endometrial
calcification which may be obtained as an outpatient.

NOTIFICATION:  The findings were discussed with ___, M.D. by
___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 11:56 am, 10 minutes
after discovery of the findings.

## Radiology Examinations [2127-08-28 07:48:00]
- Note_Type: RR
- Exam_Name: ['MR HEAD W & W/O CONTRAST']
- Text: EXAMINATION:  MR HEAD W AND W/O CONTRAST T9112 MR HEAD.

INDICATION:  History: ___ with spinal cord lesions identified on OSH MRI,
bilateral ___ numbness, requested by neurology// Please obtain MRI on 3T
scanner with/without contrast to evaluate for any lesions, demyelinating
process.

TECHNIQUE:  Sagittal and axial T1 weighted imaging were performed. After
administration of 6 mL of Gadavist intravenous contrast, axial imaging was
performed with gradient echo, FLAIR, diffusion, and T1 technique. Sagittal
MPRAGE imaging was performed and re-formatted in axial and coronal
orientations.

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

There are several FLAIR and T1 hypointense/T2 hyperintense vertically
oriented, predominantly periventricular white matter lesions.  There is no
associated enhancement after contrast administration.  Findings favor a
demyelinating process, with the T1/FLAIR hypointensity of the lesions
suggesting a chronic process.  The posterior fossa is unremarkable.  The
ventricles and sulci are slightly prominent for patient's age, suggesting mild
cortical volume loss.

There is no evidence of hemorrhage, edema, midline shift or infarction.

The ventricles and sulci are age appropriate.
Major vascular flow voids are preserved.  Major dural venous sinuses are
patent.

The paranasal sinuses and mastoid air cells are clear.  The orbits appear
unremarkable.

IMPRESSION:


1. Nonenhancing supratentorial white matter lesions predominantly in the
periventricular white matter bilaterally are suggestive of a demyelinating
condition such as multiple sclerosis.  T1/FLAIR hypointensity of the lesions
suggests chronicity.
2. Diffuse prominent ventricles and sulci suggesting cortical volume loss.
3. No evidence of intracranial hemorrhage or territorial infarction.

## Radiology Examinations [2180-09-02 00:04:00]
- Note_Type: RR
- Exam_Name: ['CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST']
- Text: EXAMINATION:  CT abdomen and pelvis.

INDICATION:  NO_PO contrast; History: ___ with epigastric abdominal pain, very
anxious and requesting scanNO_PO contrast// Please evaluate for obstruction,
cholecystitis, biliary obstruction, AAA

TECHNIQUE:  Single phase split bolus contrast: MDCT axial images were acquired
through the abdomen following intravenous contrast administration with split
bolus technique.
Oral contrast was not administered.
Coronal and sagittal reformations were performed and reviewed on PACS.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 6.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 31.3 mGy (Body) DLP =
15.6 mGy-cm.
   2) Spiral Acquisition 6.2 s, 49.1 cm; CTDIvol = 10.0 mGy (Body) DLP = 492.6
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 508 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

LOWER CHEST: Visualized lung fields are within normal limits.  There is no
evidence of pleural or pericardial effusion.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
There is no evidence of focal lesions. There is no evidence of intrahepatic or
extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  There is gallbladder wall edema, with mild
gallbladder distention.  No significant pericholecystic fat stranding.  No
radiopaque gallstones are identified.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation.  There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS: The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

free fluid.

disease is noted.

## Discharge [2203-02-12 00:00:00]
- Note_Type: DS
- Text:  
Name:  ___               Unit No:   ___
 
Admission Date:  ___              Discharge Date:   ___
 
Date of Birth:  ___             Sex:   F
 
Service: NEUROLOGY
 
Allergies: 
Penicillins / Cephalosporins / Ciprofloxacin
 
Attending: ___.
 
Chief Complaint:
left knee pain
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None
 
History of Present Illness:
___ is a ___ year-old disabled, MR patient with 
refractory epilepsy and pan-hypopituitarism who is followed here 
by Dr. ___. At baseline, she is verbal, bed/wheelchair-bound 
(can transfer with 1-man assitance bed-to-chair) and lives in a 
group home with visits from her parents. Her group home 
contacted ___ (see OMR note ___ re. decreased ability 
to transfer and leg pain with leg giving out starting last 
___. Her appointment was moved up to this coming ___, 
and Ortho evaluation of the leg was advised. Today, they called 
Dr. ___ the ___ service) with continued 
concern about her inability to transfer; apparently she fell or 
slumped over in the last few days with the toilet and is 
requiring additional
assistance with transfers. She was advised to come to the ED for 
evaluation.
She arrived with her mother, who seems to think that the leg 
problem is pain-related (patient endorses this, and says that 
her knee hurts; not sure why and neigther is her mother other 
than pointing out a superficial bruise on the patella). The 
mother said that her main concern was not the leg, but rather 
her
perception that the pt is "over-medicated" and less responsive 
today than usual, which she noticed on visiting this morning at 
the group home. The mother wants her "blood levels checked" and  
then followed up in clinic this coming week, and she would like 
to go back to the group home, where she thinks ___ can be 
best taken care of. She does not hope for inpatient admission.
The patient repeatedly expressed a desire to put her clothes on 
and eat and leave the hospital. Her only complaint or concern as 
best I can tell, is that she is hungry and wishes to leave. 
There has been no recent infection, and no witnessed convulsions 
in the past week+.
The mother does not think that seizures have anything to do with 
the leg problem. She says that ___ may have seizures all 
the time, and points out a variety of facial expressions and 
pauses and eye movements that seem subtle to me (or in some 
cases not appreciable), and says they often swipe the VNS magnet 
for such behaviors. She does not think this is a significant 
change today, and thinks that grogginess from over-medication is 
the problem. No recent infection. The only alternative idea she 
has for the ___ problems is that she may have found out 
about her favorite group-home worker leaving for health reasons 
in the near future, and may be upset about that.

 
Past Medical History:
1. right parietal astrocytoma  age ___, s/p resection and 
radiation (so baseline left hemiparesis), complicated by 
hydrocephalus s/p VP shunt
2. refractory seizures on multiple AEDs, s/p VNS; seimiology is
unclear to me; mother says she has little seizures all the time
and points out a variety of manifestations (turns red in the
face; brief movements of her eyes, brief moments of
non-responsiveness), although it is not clear to me what is the
basis for seizure versus not seizure. Mother says she swipes the
VNS magnet to activate VNS frequently for such events. Last
?generalized seizure with post-ictal period noted in OMR chart
was sometime in ___, preceeded by sometime in ___. Last VNS
update in ___. sleep apnea with obese neck; snores/wakes frequently
(including for nocturia); does not tolerate CPAP.
4. Panhypopituitarism (hypogonadism, adrenal insufficiency,
hypothyroidism); on glucocorticoid and thyroid replacement,
progesterone)
5. Depression 
6. Osteoporosis with unclear h/o knee and shoulder pain 
7. Meningiomas (Right parietal, growing @2cm; RF=XRT@youth)
8. Developmental Delay / MR
9. s/p Mohs surgery for a recurrent nodular basal cell cancer on
the left occiput; also s/p BCC Tx with Aldara.
10. h/o urinary incontinence and nocturia, chronic
11. h/o VPS in RLV, reportedly removed in ___ (but seen on
current and prior head imaging, with dilated ventricle)
12. s/p cholecystectomy in ___
 
Social History:
___
Family History:
Adopted
 
Physical Exam:
ADMISSION EXAM:
Vital signs @triage:  95.6F  64  128/77  16  98%RA
(mother says pt is "always cold")

General: Lying in bed, awake, intermittently uncooperative
(states desire to leave and eat food out with mother), NAD.
HEENT: Left old craniotomy scar, alopecia. Obese neck and snores
loudly even while awake, intermittently. Maldeveloped cranium
with bossing, hair thinned. Atraumatic. No scleral icterus.
Mucous membranes are moist. No lesions noted in oropharynx.
Mallampati IV airway.
Neck: Supple, obese. No bruits. No lymphadenopathy. No goiter.
VNS scar @chest.
Pulmonary: Good air movement in all lung fields, but distant
sounds bilaterally at bases; upper airway rhonchorous sounds
cloud ability to auscultate distally, but there may be distal
rhonchi as well. Minimally-tachypneic breathing with 
intermittent
snoring, does not appear uncomfortable.
Cardiac: RRR, distant HS, no loud M/R/G.
Abdomen: Obese, non-tender (other than ___, it hurts cause my
stomach's empty and I need to eat"), and non-distended, +
normoactive bowel sounds.
Extremities: Left knee has a small, mildly tender bruise over 
the
patella and some other minor bruising over the calf, which is
non-tender. The leg is moderately swollen, not warm or
erythematous. Warm and well-perfused, no clubbing, cyanosis, or
edema. 2+ pulses bilaterally.

*****************
Neurologic examination:

Mental Status:
Awake, alert, in NAD. Oriented to season is winter (not
year/month/etc -- mother says this is her baseline). Oriented to
"it begins with R" for name. ___ answers involving her
desire to eat and to put her clothes on and leave so that she 
can
eat -- "my stomach is empty" and "I need to eat" in response to
questions above pain etc. ROS. Speech sounds mildly dysarthric,
but mother says this is baseline. Language is fluent with
constricted affect and prosody. Comprehension is poor, but
grossly intact and per the mother at baseline. Follows some
simple midline and appendicular commands, but exam is limited by
resistence, minimal cooperation, and perseveration on leaving to
eat.

-Cranial Nerves:
II: PERRL, 3 to 2mm and brisk. Visual fields are grossly full by
blink to threat. Not compliant with fundoscopic examination,
moving continuously.
III, IV, VI: EOMs appear full and are conjugate. She has several
high-amplitude beats of horizontal nystagmus (~3 beats) with 
some
eye movements, but I cannot get her to reproduce the movements,
and it is not consistent when I have her track. Normal saccades
except for the occasional nystagmus as above.
V: Facial sensation intact to light touch and pin bilaterally
V1-V2-V3.
VII: Left lower facial weakness, more pronounced with forced
smile.
VIII: Hearing grossly intact (Left is good ear?); says she can
hear pillow-scratch bilaterally.
IX, X: Palate elevates symmetrically with phonation; limited
view.
XI: ___ equal strength in trapezii bilaterally.
XII: Tongue protrusion is midline.

-Motor:
Left UE is moderately contracted, though patient can extend the
arm somewhat, hold it anti-gravity and move wrist, fingers. 
Gives
___ triceps resistence, less than that deltoid and wrist/finger
flexors. Hypertonic/moderately spastic. RUE gives better (?full)
resistance with triceps, biceps, delt, wrist. No gross asterixis
or myoclonus. Left ataxia, but not so much on the right.
Diminished muscle bulk bilaterally, and decreased tone in the
LLE>RLE. She can move both LEs, but does not give me a full
effort and does not lift either off the bed; says that LLE/knee
hurt.

-Sensory:
Endorses +LT and pin in all four extremities, withdraws to mild
nail pressure x4.

-Reflexes (left; right):
Pec/delt (+++;++)
Biceps (+++;++)
Triceps  (+;+)
Brachioradialis (+++;++)
Quadriceps / patellar (0;0)
Gastroc-soleus / achilles (++++;+) few beats of clonus at left
ankle
Plantar response was extensor on the Left.

-Coordination:
Ataxia Left >> right with Finger-to-nose testing. Cannot do
heel-knee-shin. Bilateral dysdiadochokinesia, much worse on the
paretic Left. 

-Gait: mother insisted that I do not try to get her up to
transfer unless I have "4 hefty men" to help.
-------------

Discharge Exam:
More awake and alert than yesterday.  She follows simple 
commands, answers questions appropriately, and has fluent speech 
without paraphasic errors.  She was not tachypneic today when 
conversing.  Pupils equal, resting exotropia but EOMI without 
nystagmus.  No obvious dysarthria.  Spasticity and weakness on 
left side as before.  One beat of ankle clonus on left, none on 
right.
 
Pertinent Results:
___ 03:45PM   WBC-5.5 RBC-4.50 HGB-13.6 HCT-41.2 MCV-92 
MCH-30.2 MCHC-33.0 RDW-13.8
___ 03:45PM   NEUTS-67.3 ___ MONOS-4.4 EOS-1.4 
BASOS-1.5
___ 03:45PM   PLT COUNT-177
___ 03:45PM   GLUCOSE-80 UREA N-23* CREAT-1.1 SODIUM-144 
POTASSIUM-4.9 CHLORIDE-107 TOTAL CO2-26 ANION GAP-16
___ 06:10PM URINE  BLOOD-NEG NITRITE-NEG PROTEIN-NEG 
GLUCOSE-NEG KETONE-NEG BILIRUBIN-NEG UROBILNGN-NEG PH-7.0 
LEUK-NEG

___ ___
IMPRESSION: 
1. Stable appearance of dense lesion in the right parietal 
region felt to be represent a meningioma. Also, no significant 
change in right frontal and right occipital dural-based lesions. 

2. Stable moderate dilatation of the lateral ventricles, right 
more than 
left, with the ventriculostomy catheter terminating in the left 
frontal horn. 

___ Hip/Knee/Ankle XR - no fracture

___ ___ Doppler (left leg) - no DVT

___ CXR - 
Mild cardiomegaly and mediastinal vascular engorgement are 
unchanged.  
Pulmonary vasculature is mildly engorged, but this could be an 
effect of 
supine positioning. There are no focal findings to suggest 
pneumonia or large 
scale aspiration. No pleural effusion or pneumothorax. 

UA negative

___ Video swallow
IMPRESSION: Normal oropharyngeal swallowing videofluoroscopy.

___ MRI Head c/s contrast
The ventriculostomy via the right transfrontal approach has been 

better characterized in the recent CT. The markedly dilated 
ventricular 
configuration, right greater than left, is unchanged compared to 
prior CT 
studies.  
  
Along the right parietal convexity, there is an avidly enhancing 
rounded 
dural-based lesion, measuring approximately 2.3 x 1.9 cm, 
representing a 
meningioma. A smaller right posterior parasagittal rounded 
dural-based lesion  (image 11:8) also represents a meningioma. 
There are flat dural-based lesions  with similar avid 
post-contrast enhancement in the right parasagittal frontal  
convexity (image 11:19), the left infrafrontal convexity (image 
11:12) and a  tiny one in the left frontal parafalcine region, 
compatible with en plaque  meningiomas. The multiplicity of 
meningiomas in light of ___ history of  radiation suggests 
that these are most likely radiation-induced meningiomas. There 
are burr holes in both sides of the frontal andparietal regions. 
There is no abnormal restricted diffusion to suggest acute 
ischemia. IMPRESSION: 1. Multiple avidly enhancing dural-based 
lesions, compatible with meningiomas. Given the history of 
whole-brain radiation, these may represent radiation-induced 
meningiomas. 2. Unchanged dilated ventricular configuration, 
with right transfrontal ventriculostomy drain. 3. No acute 
ischemia. 

EEG: Final report pending. EEG showed diffuse attenuation with 
superimposed moderate voltage beta activity, evolving to 
repetitive spikes over the left parietal region, then diffuse 
spike and wave activity.  The seizures lasted between 30 and 90 
seconds.  Otherwise, she appears to be at her baseline.
 
Brief Hospital Course:
___ h/o epilepsy related to resected R parietal astrocytoma 
s/p VNS and R frontal meningioma p/w difficulty transferring 
to/from bed.

[] Left Knee Pain - The patient presented with report of 
difficulty with transfers, possibly due to pain from her left 
knee with some initial evidence of mild bruising. She was 
described as not putting weight on her legs and not locking her 
knees. Fracture and DVT were excluded with imaging. Orthopedics 
evaluated her knee and detected the presence of what is likely a 
___ cyst. They did not feel an acute intervention or imaging 
was needed, but did suggest an MRI of the knee as an outpatient 
if the pain persists. An AFO for the left ankle (foot drop) was 
recommended and ordered.

[] Epilepsy - The patient did not have any increased frequency 
of seizures reported, but she did have a relatively high 
frequency of electrographic and clinical seizures noted on 
continuous video EEG. Her Zonisamide was increased to 300 mg 
QHS. The semiologies included [1] right arm myoclonus with left 
arm extensor posturing, loss of awareness, with post-ictal 
lethargy, and [2] arm extension, loss of awareness.

[ ] Low grade temperatures - The patient had two low grade 
temperatures to 100.1-100.2. Her stress dose hydrocortisone 
protocol was initiated and an infectious workup was pursued. 
However, the fever resolved without intervention, likely 
representing aspiration pneumonitis in the setting of likely 
aspiration while her level of awareness was altered by her 
seizures.

[ ] Performing MRI - Of note, there were concerns about how to 
perform the MRI regarding the ___ VNS stimulator and 
concerns about "metal" related to the ventriculoperitoneal shunt

INDICATION:  ___ with ruq pain and tenderness// evaluate for biliary pathology

TECHNIQUE:  Grey scale and color Doppler ultrasound images of the abdomen were
obtained.

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

LIVER: The liver contains innumerable cysts compatible with polycystic liver
disease.  The contour of the liver is smooth.  There is no focal liver mass. 
The main portal vein is patent with hepatopetal flow.  There is no ascites.

BILE DUCTS: There is no intrahepatic biliary dilation.  The CHD measures 4 mm.

GALLBLADDER: There is no evidence of stones or gallbladder wall thickening.

PANCREAS: The imaged portion of the pancreas appears within normal limits,
without masses or pancreatic ductal dilation, with portions of the pancreatic
tail obscured by overlying bowel gas.

SPLEEN: Normal echogenicity, measuring 6.8 cm.

KIDNEYS: The right kidney measures 15.1 cm.  The left kidney measures 13.5 cm.
Bilateral renal cortices are echogenic and contain innumerable cysts
compatible with polycystic kidney disease.  There is no evidence of masses,
stones, or hydronephrosis in the kidneys.

RETROPERITONEUM:  The visualized portions of aorta and IVC are within normal
limits.

IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of cholecystitis or biliary ductal dilation.
2. Innumerable cysts in the liver and kidneys compatible with polycystic
kidney and liver disease.

## Radiology Examinations [2143-03-31 23:36:00]
- Note_Type: RR
- Exam_Name: ['CTA CHEST ', 'CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST']
- Text: EXAMINATION:  CT torso

INDICATION:  ___ with chest/ abd pain, intractable vomiting, PCKD with marked
HTN-- note she does not have an IV contrast dye allergy, she has only had an
unrelated allergic reaction once to shellfish// eval aortic dissection

TECHNIQUE:  Axial multidetector CT images were obtained through the thorax
after the uneventful administration of intravenous contrast in the arterial
phase.  Then, imaging was obtained through the abdomen and pelvis in the
portal venous phase. Reformatted coronal and sagittal images through the
chest, abdomen, and pelvis, and oblique maximal intensity projection images of
the chest were submitted to PACS and reviewed.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Stationary Acquisition 0.5 s, 1.0 cm; CTDIvol = 1.2 mGy (Body) DLP = 1.2
mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 2.0 s, 1.0 cm; CTDIvol = 4.6 mGy (Body) DLP = 4.6
mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 9.4 s, 35.9 cm; CTDIvol = 5.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 175.1
mGy-cm.
   4) Spiral Acquisition 14.8 s, 51.0 cm; CTDIvol = 6.1 mGy (Body) DLP = 302.8
mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Body) = 511 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

CHEST:

HEART AND VASCULATURE: Pulmonary vasculature is well opacified to the
subsegmental level without filling defect to indicate a pulmonary embolus. 
The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without evidence of dissection or
intramural hematoma.  The heart, pericardium, and great vessels are within
normal limits. No pericardial effusion is seen.

AXILLA, HILA, AND MEDIASTINUM: No axillary, mediastinal, or hilar
lymphadenopathy is present.  No mediastinal mass.

PLEURAL SPACES: No pleural effusion or pneumothorax.

LUNGS/AIRWAYS: There is moderate bilateral centrilobular pulmonary emphysema
and paraseptal pulmonary emphysema primarily in bilateral lung apices.  Lungs
are clear without masses or areas of parenchymal opacification.  The airways
are patent to the level of the segmental bronchi bilaterally.

BASE OF NECK: Visualized portions of the base of the neck show no abnormality.

ABDOMEN:

HEPATOBILIARY: The liver demonstrates homogenous attenuation throughout. 
Multiple hypodense lesions are noted within the liver consistent with cysts,
the largest measures 2.8 cm in the right hepatic lobe.  Multiple subcentimeter
hypodense lesions are also noted within the liver, too small to characterize
but likely represents biliary cysts or hamartomas.  There is no evidence of
intrahepatic or extrahepatic biliary dilatation.  The gallbladder is within
normal limits.

PANCREAS: The pancreas has normal attenuation throughout, without evidence of
focal lesions or pancreatic ductal dilatation. There is no peripancreatic
stranding.

SPLEEN: The spleen shows normal size and attenuation throughout, without
evidence of focal lesions.

ADRENALS:  The right and left adrenal glands are normal in size and shape.

URINARY: The kidneys are of normal and symmetric size with normal nephrogram. 
No hydronephrosis.  Of note, while this is not a dedicated exam for assessing
renal lesions, no concerning renal mass is identified.  Innumerable cysts of
intermediate density in bilateral kidneys are noted, incompletely
characterized.  There is no perinephric abnormality.

GASTROINTESTINAL: The stomach is unremarkable.  Evaluation of the bowel is
limited by lack of mesenteric fat.  Small bowel loops demonstrate normal
caliber, wall thickness, and enhancement throughout.  There appears to be wall
thickening and edema of the cecum and proximal ascending colon consistent with
colitis.  The appendix is not visualized.  There is no free intraperitoneal
fluid or free air.

PELVIS:

The urinary bladder and distal ureters are unremarkable.  There is no free
fluid in the pelvis.

REPRODUCTIVE ORGANS: The uterus is unremarkable.  In the left adnexa, there is
a 5.3 x 7.3 cm cystic structure which may represent a peritoneal inclusion
cyst, paraovarian cyst, or adnexal cyst.

LYMPH NODES: Assessment is limited without PO contrast or mesenteric fat. 
Within this limitation: There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric
lymphadenopathy.  There is no pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

VASCULAR: There is no abdominal aortic aneurysm.  Mild atherosclerotic disease
is noted.

BONES AND SOFT TISSUES: There is no evidence of worrisome osseous lesions or
acute fracture.  The abdominal and pelvic wall is within normal limits.

IMPRESSION:


1. No pulmonary embolism or aortic dissection.
2. Wall thickening of the cecum and proximal colon consistent with colitis. 
No focal collections or obstruction.
3. 7.3 cm left benign-appearing pelvic cystic lesion may represent a
peritoneal inclusion cyst, paraovarian cyst, or adnexal cyst.  Correlate with
prior imaging or history.
4. Innumerable renal cysts of intermediate density are incompletely
characterized, compatible with known history of PCKD.

- Note_Type: RR
- Exam_Name: ['CTA ABD & PELVIS', 'CTA CHEST W&W/O C&RECONS, NON-CORONARY']
- Text: HISTORY:  Severe left lower quadrant abdominal pain radiating to back.  Please
evaluate for aortic dissection.

TECHNIQUE:  Axial helical MDCT images were obtained through the torso in the
early arterial phase after administration of 80 cc of Visipaque intravenous
contrast material.  Multiplanar reformatted images in coronal, sagittal and
curved reformatted images were obtained.

DLP 564.09 mGy per cm.

COMPARISON:  MRI abdomen from ___, abdominal ultrasound from ___ and CT abdomen and pelvis from ___.

FINDINGS:

CT Thorax:  The thyroid is unremarkable.  There is no supraclavicular lymph
node enlargement.  The airways are patent of the subsegmental level.  There is
no hilar, mediastinal or axillary lymph node enlargement by CT size criteria. 
There is cardiomegaly.  There is coronary artery calcification and mitral
anulus calcification.  

Lung windows demonstrate bilateral pleural effusions, moderate on the right
and small on the left.  There is interstitial septal thickening and
ground-glass opacities consistent with pulmonary edema.  No pneumothorax is
present.  There is a hiatal hernia.

CTA torso:  The aorta and main thoracic vessels are well opacified.  There is
no evidence of aortic dissection or intramural hematoma.  There is diffuse
atherosclerotic calcification and plaque of the thoracic and abdominal aorta. 
The abdominal aorta is ectatic measuring 2.1 cm in greatest dimension
unchanged from prior exam.  The pulmonary arteries are opacified to the
segmental level.  There is no filling defect seen in the main, right, left,
lobar or segmental pulmonary arteries.

CT abdomen:  The liver does not demonstrate focal lesions.  There is
pneumobilia unchanged from prior exam likely related to patient's prior
sphincterotomy.  The patient is status post cholecystectomy.  There is
extrahepatic biliary dilatation with the common bile duct measuring 14 mm with
a stone seen within the distal CBD.  The CBD dilatation and stone are
unchanged in size and configuration from prior exams.  The pancreas is
atrophic.  The spleen is unremarkable.  The adrenal glands are unremarkable. 
The right kidney demonstrates a 2 cm hypodense cyst with minimal dependent
calcification along known margin unchanged from prior exams.  The left kidney
is atrophic.  There is a 2 cm lesion in the inferior pole of the left kidney
which is larger from ___.  It is not clearly simple.

The stomach, duodenum and small bowel are unremarkable.  Patient status post
right hemicolectomy.  The colon is otherwise unremarkable.  There is a right
ventral abdominal wall hernia with a nonobstructive loop of small bowel within
it.  There is no retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymph node enlargement by CT
size criteria.

CT pelvis:  The bladder is decompressed with a Foley catheter are seen.  The
uterus is not seen.  There is no pelvic wall are inguinal lymphadenopathy. 
There are bilateral fat containing inguinal hernias.

Osseous structures:  No suspicious lytic or blastic osseous lesion is seen. 
There is a stabilization rod in the right proximal femur.  A stable sclerotic
focus inthe midline of the sacrum again noted.  A vertebral body compression
is seen at L2 which is unchanged from prior exams.

focal
