Complications: None. Notably, upon lying in a flat supine position, the
patient's blood pressure decreased from 151/55 mmHg to 81/32 mmHg with
associated symptoms of feeling faint, nauseous and diaphoretic. Staff
positioned the patient onto her side with an increase of her blood pressure to
104/41 mmHg and full resolution of her symptoms without any further incident
or complaints throughout the remainder of the study.


1) Structure: Axial dual-inversion T1-weighted images of the myocardium were
obtained without spectral fat saturation pre-pulses in 5-mm contiguous slices.
2) Function: Breath-hold cine SSFP images were acquired in the left
ventricular 2-chamber, 4-chamber, horizontal long axis, short axis slices (8-
mm slices with 2-mm gaps), sagittal and coronal orientations of the left
ventricular outflow tract, and aortic valve short axis orientations.
3) Flow: Phase-contrast cine images were obtained transverse to the aorta
(axial plane) and main pulmonary artery (oblique plane).

Findings:
Structure and Function
There was normal epicardial fat distribution.  The pericardial thickness was
normal. There was a very small pericardial effusion with no pleural effusions.
The origins of the left main (3 o'clock take-off) and right (12 o'clock take-
off) coronary arteries were identified in their customary positions without
anomalous course.  The indexed diameter of the ascending thoracic aorta was
mildly dilated. The aorta measured 37mm (sinus), 29mm (sino-tubular ridge,
preserved without effacement), and 39mm (ascending). The distance from the
aortic valve leaflet to the point of maximal ascending aorta dilation measured
62mm. The indexed diameter of the descending thoracic aorta was normal.  The
main pulmonary artery diameter index was normal.  The left atrial AP dimension
was normal.  The right and left atrial lengths in the 4-chamber view were
normal.  The coronary sinus diameter was normal.
The left ventricular end-diastolic dimension index was normal.  The end-
diastolic volume index was mildly increased.  The calculated left ventricular
ejection fraction was normal at 67% with normal regional systolic function.
The anteroseptal and inferolateral wall thicknesses were normal. The left
ventricular mass index was mildly increased.  The right ventricular end-
diastolic volume index was normal.  The calculated right ventricular ejection
fraction was normal at 64%, with normal free wall motion.
The aortic valve has moderate to severe stenosis and appears unicuspid,
however partial fusion of leaflets with resulting bicuspid morphology cannot
be excluded. A systolic signal void was seen in the proximal ascending aorta
consistent with aortic stenosis. A diastolic signal void was seen in the left
ventricular outflow tract consistent with aortic regurgitation.

Quantitative Flow
 Due to high flow velocity across the aortic valve with aliasing, the aortic
total flow and stroke volume, any aortic regurgitation or mitral
regurgitation, any intra-cardiac shunt, and cardiac output were not able to be
quantified. The right ventricular stroke volume and pulmonic flow demonstrated
moderate tricuspid regurgitation with no significant pulmonic regurgitation.

Non-Cardiac Findings
None.

Impression:
1. Moderate to severe aortic valve stenosis with unicuspid appearance, however
partial fusion of leaflets with resulting bicuspid morphology cannot be
excluded.
2. The indexed diameter of the ascending thoracic aorta is mildly increased.
The indexed diameters of the descending thoracic aorta and main pulmonary
artery were normal.
3. Normal left ventricular cavity size with normal regional left ventricular
systolic function.  The LVEF was normal at 67%.
4. Normal right ventricular cavity size and systolic function. The RVEF was
normal at 64%.

after discharge on ___ from medical service for hypertensive

worked to control her blood pressure well and had achieved 
adequate glucose control. She was walking at home, and tripped 
over a rug, fell and hit her head. She subsequently lost 
consciousness. Prior to the fall, there was no syncopal or 
presyncopal episode. Of note, per report, she had missed her 
dialysis that day, although the patient denies this on 
questioning. She normally receives dialysis ___, and ___ at 
___.
.
Her initial trauma workup has thus far been negative for 
fracture. She is currently eating/drinking without any issues. 
Nephrology/Dialysis is following the patient for both her HD and 
hypertension. She received her dialysis today (___). 
.
The patient's home PO medications were held on admission, 
including her HTN meds. Over night, she received 25 units of 
regular insulin within two hours and haldol 0.5 mg IM. She 
became hypoglycemic and developed altered mental status. A 
transfer to the medicine service was then requested for altered 
mental status. 

 
Past Medical History:

blood shot.

HEAD CT: No acute intracranial abnormality. Right frontal 
cephalohematoma. 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
Asssessment and Plan:
___ year old female with MMP, including ESRD on HD, HTN, and 
DMII, poor compliance at home, presented after fall w/o LOC, now 
transfered to medicine with negative w/u for trauma and 
hypoglycemia and AMS.
.
# s/p fall: details unclear.  Possibly a mechanical fall since 
there were reports of tripping on a rug.  However, hypoglycemia 
could not be ruled out.  She was originally admitted to the 
trauma team and workup including head CT was negative.  She did 
sustain right periorbital bruising and a right subconjunctival 
hemorrage.  ___ saw her during this admission and recommended 
that she goes to rehab because of her unsteadiness.  She 
initially agreed to go, but because of her social situations 
(see below), she left against medical advice.  The risks of 
going home, including repeated falls because of her 
unsteadiness, were explained to her.  She was told that if she 
does choose to go home AMA, she should use her wheelchair for 
mobility and to have help at home for transfer and stairs.
.
# DM: Labile blood sugars.  She had an episode of hypoglycemia 
because 2 insulin doses were given within short period of time, 
but she recovered with dextrose.  Her lantus was then started.  
She was monitored overnight and her fingersticks were high.  
However, since she has a problem with non-compliance and has had 
hypoglycemic episodes during the past 2 admissions, we favor 
keeping her on a more liberal sliding scale than an aggressive 
one.  She was discharged on 4 units of lantus at night and a 
sliding scale.
.
# SOCIAL SITUATION: She has a number of suboptimal social 
situations.  She is currently living with her mother and sister 
whom she seems not to get along with.  She also expresses 
concern/paranoia about her family stealing her methadone at home 
when she is at the hospital, which was why she wanted to leave 
AMA.  Social work saw her urgently in the morning but she was 
adamant about leaving.  She is also applying for housing which 
is another stressor for her.  Because of these social issues, it 
seems like she is unable to focus on her current medical 
problems.  She admits that she takes her medications and insulin 
only about half the time.
.
# HTN: h/o difficult to control htn.  Here she was initially 
hypertensive to 180's, but then dropped to high 90's possibly 
from getting all her meds restarted.  Her heart rate was in the 
50's.  We recommended that she stop her metoprolol and keep her 
other antihypertensives, but she left AMA before we can give her 
the formal paperwork.
.
# ESRD: Dialysis as her outpt schedule.
.
# Narcotic Use: methadone 20 mg TID 
.
# HCV: LFTs were checked during last admission on ___ and were 
WNL.

Home with Service

Discharged AMA
 
Discharge Condition:
Discharged AMA
 
Discharge Instructions:
Discharged AMA
 
Followup Instructions:
___

| RR | ['HIP (UNILAT 2 VIEW) W/PELVIS (1 VIEW) LEFT'] | EXAMINATION:  HIP (UNILAT 2 VIEW) W/PELVIS (1 VIEW) LEFT

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with left hip pain status post fall.

TECHNIQUE:  Frontal view radiograph of the pelvis with additional frontal and
cross-table views of the left hip.

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

There is a proximal left femoral fracture. The lateral fracture line extends
obliquely below the level of the greater trochanter. The medial aspect of the
fracture line extends through the level of the lesser trochanter.  The
femoroacetabular joint is well aligned.  The distal fragment is displaced
medially and anteriorly. Degenerative changes are noted about the lumbar
spine. Calcified atherosclerotic disease is incidentally noted.

IMPRESSION: 

Reverse obliquity intertrochanteric displaced left femoral fracture with mild
overriding.
 |
| RR | ['LUMBO-SACRAL SPINE (AP & LAT)'] | EXAMINATION:  LUMBO-SACRAL SPINE (AP AND LAT)

INDICATION:  ___ s/p unwitnessed fall now presenting with left hip pain,
shortening, rotation.

TECHNIQUE:  Frontal and lateral view radiographs of the lumbar spine.

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

5 non-rib-bearing lumbar vertebral bodies are present.  There is
dextroscoliosis.  The vertebral bodies are well aligned.  There is a subtle
loss of vertebral height at the left lateral aspect of L2, presumably chronic.
Degenerative changes are noted including endplate osteophyte formation and
loss of intervertebral disc height.  No fracture or subluxation is seen.

IMPRESSION: 

No acute fracture or subluxation.  Chronic degenerative changes.
 |
| RR | ['CHEST (SINGLE VIEW)'] | EXAMINATION:  Chest: Frontal views.

INDICATION:  ___ s/p unwitnessed fall now with left hip pain, shortening,
rotation. Evaluate for acute process.

TECHNIQUE:  Chest: Frontal views.

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

The lungs are clear without focal consolidation. No pleural effusion or
pneumothorax is seen.  Cardiac and mediastinal silhouettes are unremarkable. 
An azygos fissure is seen overlying the right upper lung apex with a prominent
azygos vein along its inferior portion.  Prominent costochondral
calcifications are seen.  Levoscoliosis of the thoracic spine.

IMPRESSION: 

No acute cardiopulmonary process.

| RR | ['CT HEAD W/O CONTRAST'] | EXAMINATION:  CT HEAD W/O CONTRAST

INDICATION:  ___ s/p unwitnessed fall now w left hip pain, shortening,
rotation// ?acute process

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous axial images from skullbase to vertex were obtained
without intravenous contrast.  Coronal and sagittal reformats were also
performed.

DOSE:   Acquisition sequence:
   1) Sequenced Acquisition 8.0 s, 17.4 cm; CTDIvol = 46.2 mGy (Head) DLP =
802.7 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Head) = 803 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

There is prominence of the ventricles and sulci compatible with global volume
loss.  Focal hypodensities in the cerebellar hemispheres are compatible with
chronic infarcts.  Encephalomalacia in the left frontal lobe is also noted. 
Confluent periventricular and subcortical white matter hypodensities are
likely sequela of chronic small vessel disease.  There is no intra-axial or
extra-axial hemorrhage, mass effect, midline shift, or acute major vascular
territorial infarct.

Included paranasal sinuses and mastoids are clear besides mild mucosal
thickening in the right sphenoid sinus.  Skull and extracranial soft tissues
are unremarkable.

IMPRESSION: 

No acute intracranial process, no hemorrhage.
Global volume loss, white matter dense hypodensities which are likely sequela
of chronic small vessel disease.  Bilateral chronic cerebellar infarcts and
left frontal encephalomalacia.

intervertebral disc height loss, posterior osteophytes, and uncovertebral
joint hypertrophy spanning C3-4 through C6-7 resulting in up to mild canal
narrowing is and mild-to-moderate foraminal narrowing, worst at C5-6
bilaterally and C6-7 on the left.There is no prevertebral edema.

The thyroid is unremarkable.  Calcified pleural based biapical scarring is
noted.

IMPRESSION: 

Degenerative changes without fracture or malalignment.

INDICATION:  History: ___ with stroke, intubation.  Evaluate endotracheal
tube.

TECHNIQUE:  Portable supine chest radiograph

COMPARISON:  Outside hospital chest radiograph

FINDINGS: 

Endotracheal tube tip terminates 5 cm from the carina.  Enteric tube
terminates in the left upper quadrant.  Lung volumes are low, accentuating the
pulmonary vasculature and cardiac contour.  Accounting for this there is mild

| RR | ['CTA NECK W&W/OC & RECONS', 'CTA HEAD W&W/O C & RECONS'] | EXAMINATION:  CTA HEAD AND CTA NECK PQ147 CT HEADNECK

INDICATION:  History: ___ with stroke, intubation // evaluate stroke, acute
process, bleed, evaluate for interval change from outside imaging

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous axial images were obtained through the brain without
contrast material. Subsequently, rapid axial imaging was performed from the
aortic arch through the brain during infusion of 70 mL of Omnipaque
intravenous contrast material. Three-dimensional angiographic volume rendered,
curved reformatted and segmented images were generated. This report is based
on interpretation of all of these images.

DOSE:   Total DLP (Head) = 4,195 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  Outside CT of the head from ___.

FINDINGS: 

Head CT:  Hypodensity within the bilateral occipital lobes, right greater the
left:  Within the left cerebellar cortex, and within the pons and midbrain
consistent with acute infarction.  No evidence of hemorrhagic conversion or
midline shift.  Hyperdensity within the mid to cephalad basal artery
consistent with thrombosis.  Normal ventricles orbits and calvarium.

Head CTA:  Absent filling of the mid to cephalad basal artery and bilateral P1
segments of the posterior cerebral arteries consistent with thrombosis. 
Reconstitution of the more peripheral posterior cerebral arteries.  Contrast
opacification of the caudal reason for current and the vertebral arteries.

Neck CTA:  Calcified and noncalcified atherosclerosis of the right carotid
bulb with approximately 50% luminal narrowing by NASCET criteria.  Calcified
and noncalcified atherosclerosis of the left carotid  bulb with approximately
28% stenosis in the left internal carotid artey  by NASCET criteria.
Other: The visualized portion of the lungs are clear. The visualized portion
of the thyroid gland is within normal limits. There is no lymphadenopathy by
CT size criteria.  Appropriately placed endotracheal tube.  Enteric tube
traverses the pharynx and visualized esophagus.  Calcific coronary artery
atherosclerosis.

IMPRESSION:


1. Absent filling of the mid to cephalad basilar artery and the bilateral P1
segment posterior cerebral arteries consistent with thrombosis. Associated
infarction within the bilateral occipital cortices at left cerebellar cortex
without hemorrhagic conversion.
2. Bilateral carotid bulb atherosclerosis with luminal narrowing, as
described.
3. Coronary artery atherosclerosis.


NOTIFICATION:  Findings discussed with Dr. ___ by Dr. ___ telephone
at 04:50 on ___, upon discovery.

trisegmentectomy in ___ with complicated course notable 
for prolonged bile leak from cut surface of liver requiring PTBD 
placement, ___ drain placement, and ERCP/stent. she has had 
several hospitalizations for investigation of drains and failure 
to thrive/poor nutrition. She now presents with dizziness, 
weakness and poor PO intake. 
.
During this admission, the left PTBD was exchanged. Initial 
cholangiogram did not show contrast in the left system. The PTBD 
was exchanged resulting in opacification. The drain was left 
open following the procedure, and then was capped with no 
significant increase in output to the pigtail drain. 24 hours 
after capping the LFTs have remained stable, patient was 
afebrile and no increase in abdominal pain.
.
Nutrition has assessed the patient with findings of severe 
malnutrition. At the time of the drain exchange, a feeding tube 
was placed which inadvertently came out later the same day. The 
following day a feeding tube was placed under fluoro and tube 
feeds have been started with goal of cycling over 18 hours.
.

FINDINGS:

lateral view, not substantiated on the frontal view, could be due to overlap
of vascular structures, versus possibly atelectasis.  No pleural effusion or
pneumothorax is seen.  Cardiomediastinal silhouette and hila are unremarkable.
Enteric tube courses inferiorly beyond the diaphragm and out of view.  PTBD
and CBD stent overlie the right upper abdomen.

- Text: INDICATION:  History: ___ with PTBD that has withdrawn from site// drain
dislodged?

TECHNIQUE:  Upright and supine views of the abdomen.

COMPARISON:  ___.

FINDINGS: 

Compared to the prior examination, the internal external PTBD which was
previously coursing through the CBD stent has been retracted with the tip
projecting over the superior most aspect of the CBD stent.  Pigtail drainage
catheter projecting over the right upper quadrant seen on prior
cross-sectional imaging at the margin of the right hepatectomy is unchanged in
position.  A Dobhoff tube tip is likely pyloric in location.  There is
relative paucity of small bowel gas, though there is no evidence for
obstruction.  High-density material is seen throughout the colon, likely prior
enteric contrast.  There is moderate colonic fecal load.  No free air is seen.

IMPRESSION: 

Retraction of the internal external PTBD with the tip now projecting over the
superior most aspect of the CBD stent.

___ year old F with Angelman's syndrome and baseline mental 
retardation developed tachypnea at group home on day of 
admission.  911 was called and she was taken to an OSH ED at 
4PM.  Per her caretakers, she had spiked a fever to ___ F on the 
day PTA and seemed lethargic.  On the day of admission, she 
remained in bed until 2PM and then was noted to be tachypnic.  
Upon arrival to the ER, she had sinus tach to 160s.  EKG 
revealed ST elevation in V2-V5.  She was given Lopressor 5 mg IV

BP then fell to 68/43 with HR 120s, satting 92% 4L NC.  She was 
given IVF bolus and started on dobutamine before being

.

proximal LAD.  No intervention was performed as the infarction 
was likely several days old with the myocardium no long viable.  
She was in cardiogenic shock with cardiac index of 1.7 and PCWP 
27. A Balloon pump placed.  BP was labile and decreased with 
versed; she was started on levophed.  She was satting 100% on

.  
Due to her MR, we were unable to obtain ROS directly from the 
patient.  However, per her caretakers, she had increased

had a UTI few weeks ago and was treated with a course of Bactrim 
and then cirpofloxacin for ten days.  Her psychiatrist recently

.

___ Influenza DFA: negative
___ Blood cultures: NGTD 
.
EKG demonstrated sinus tach at 123 with Left axis, incomplete 
RBBB, ST elevation in II, V2-V5, TWI I, aVL. 
.
___ CARDIAC CATHETERIZATION:
1.  Selective coronary angiography of this left dominant system 
revealed 
single vessel coronary artery disease.  The ___ had no 
angiographically 
significant coronary artery disease.  The LAD had a total 
proximal 
occlusion, with distal vessel injection revealing an atretic 
vessel with 
contrast pooling in the myocardium. 
2.  Resting hemodynamics revealed elevated left and right sided 
filling 
pressures with RVEDP of 16 mm Hg and PCWP mean of 25 mm Hg.  
There was 
mild pulmonary arterial hypertension of 39/29 mm Hg.  There was 
evidence 
of cardiogenic shock with a cardiac index of 1.8 l/min/m2.  
There was 
systemic arterial hypotension of 96/76 mm Hg. 
3.  Left ventriculogrpahy was deferred. 
4. Distal injection past the occusion in LAD consistent with 
myocardial 
infarction of greater than 48 hours duration with non-viable 
myocardium. 
5. Placement of IABP for cardiogenic shock 
6. Difficult initial access requiring use of smart needle to 
access LFA. 
FINAL DIAGNOSIS:     
1. Single vessel coronary artery disease. 
2. Cardiogenic shock. 
3. Systemic hypotension. 
4. Placement of IABP 
.
___ TTE: EF 25%
The left atrium is normal in size. No atrial septal defect is 
seen by 2D or color Doppler. Left ventricular wall thicknesses 
are normal. The left ventricular cavity size is normal. There is 
severe regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with 
septal, anterior and apical akinesis. The basal to mid inferior 
and lateral walls are hyperdynamic. No masses or thrombi are 
seen in the left ventricle. There is no ventricular septal 
defect. The aortic valve leaflets (3) appear structurally normal 
with good leaflet excursion and no aortic regurgitation. The 
mitral valve appears structurally normal with trivial mitral 
regurgitation. There is no mitral valve prolapse. There is mild 
pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. There is no pericardial 
effusion.  
.
___ TTE: EF 30%
The left atrium is elongated. Left ventricular wall thicknesses 
are normal. The left ventricular cavity size is top 
normal/borderline dilated. Overall left ventricular systolic 
function is moderately-to-severely depressed (LVEF= 30 %) 
secondary to akinesis of the interventricular septum and 
anterior free wall, and dyskinesis of the apex. There is no 
ventricular septal defect. Right ventricular chamber size and 
free wall motion are normal. The aortic valve leaflets (3) are 
mildly thickened but aortic stenosis is not present. No aortic 
regurgitation is seen. The mitral valve appears structurally 
normal with trivial mitral regurgitation. There is no mitral 
valve prolapse. The left ventricular inflow pattern suggests a 
restrictive filling abnormality, with elevated left atrial 
pressure. There is mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. 
There is no pericardial effusion. Compared with the findings of 
the prior study (images reviewed) of ___, the 
findings are similar.  
.
___ CXR:
There is a region of patchy alveolar infiltrate in the left 
upper lobe.  Otherwise lungs are clear. 
.
___ CXR:
1.  New mild pulmonary edema. 
2.  Left retrocardiac opacity slightly worse since last exam; 
this may 
represent atelectasis, pneumonia, or both. 
 
Brief Hospital Course:
(1) MYOCARDIAL INFARCTION
Ms. ___ is without known history of cardiac disease and 
has few cardiac risk factors.  She did start a OCP's several 
months ago, which appears to be her only risk factor for an MI.  
On cardiac catheterization from ___, she was found to have a 
100% stenosis of the LAD; no intervention was performed as it 
was thought that her MI had occurred ___ days prior to admission 
and the myocardium was dead by the time of catheterization.
.
Her course was complicated by cardiogenic shock, for which she 
was temporarily treated with an intra-aortic balloon pump, 
levophed and milrinone.  She was eventually weaned off all 
these.  She was started on aspirin, atorvastatin and digoxin, 
and OCP's were discontinued.  Low dose metoprolol tartrate and 
captopril were started once blood pressures normalized, and she 
was discharged on metoprolol succinate 100 mg PO QAM, lisinopril 
10 mg QPM, spironolactone 25 mg PO QD, and digoxin 0.125 mg PO 
daily.  She remained in sinus tachycardia with HR 100 - 120's 
for most of the admission.  
.
TTE was performed on ___ and showed an EF of 25% with 
septal, anterior and apical akinesis.  A repeat TTE was 
performed on ___ to assess for interval improvement, but 
there was no change in cardiac function.
.
She was also discharged on coumadin for prophylaxis against LV 
thrombus given the location and extent of her infarction.  INR 
was labile while in the hospital and must be followed closely as 
an out-patient until it stabilizes.
.
She was also discharged on lasix 40 mg PO QD to maintain an even 
fluid balance with her cardiac disease.
.
(2) FEVERS and LEUKOCYTOSIS
She had a low-grade temperature throughout her hospitalization.  
Prior to admission, she was treated with a full course of 
ciprofloxacin for a UTI.  Unfortunately, she was not able to 
confirm or refute localizing symptoms because of her baseline 
mental retardation, and her clinical exam never revealed a 
source of infection.  Furthermore, all blood cultures were 
negative, and urine cultures grew gram positive bacteria which 
were thought to be a colonizer (given her negative UA's and low 
CFU count).  She was put on a five day course of vancomycin 
empirically for possible line infection (it was thought that 
maybe she had transient bacteremia from the catherization 
instrumentation, even thought cultures were negative).  She was 
also put on a five day course of azithromycin for a possible 
community acquired PNA given a LUL infiltrate on CXR.  After the 
course of azithromycin, her fevers persisted and there was 
worsening of the left retrocardiac opacity.  She was started on 
a seven day course of IV cefepime and vancomycin for hospital 
acquired pneumonia.  A PICC line was placed in the left arm on 
___ for out-patient administration of IV antibiotics.
.
.
(3) POSSIBLE ANTI-PHOSPHOLIPID ANTIBODIES
It was noted that her PTT was elevated throughout her admission 
(ranging mostly in the ___ - ___) and there was concern for 
antiphospholipid antibody syndrome, especially given her MI at a 
young age and with few cardiac risk factors.  Testing for 
antiphospholipid antibodies was deferred in the in-patient 
setting because of possible false positive results with acute 
illness and anticoagulation.  This should be tested as an 
out-patient once she is off anticoagulation.
.
.
.
ISSUES FOR FOLLOW-UP:
(1) Follow PTT with possible testing for antiphospholipid 
antibodies after anticoagulation is discontinued.  Testing for 
APL antibodies was deferred during this admission because of 
concern for false results with anticoagulation and in the 
setting of acute illness.
(2) INR was labile in throughout the admission and needs to be 
followed twice a week as an out-patient until stable.
(3) OCP's were discontinued for increased clotting risk 
associated with their use.
(4) Pull PICC after completion of IV antibiotics.

Lactulose PRN

- Note_Type: RR
- Exam_Name: ['CTA NECK W&W/OC & RECONS', 'CTA HEAD W&W/O C & RECONS']
- Text: EXAMINATION:  CTA HEAD AND CTA NECK Q16 CT NECK

INDICATION:  History: ___ with intracranial hemorrhage sent from OSH// eval
vasculature and extent of hemorrhage

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous MDCT axial images were obtained through the brain
without contrast material. Subsequently, helically acquired rapid axial
imaging was performed from the aortic arch through the brain during the
infusion of intravenous contrast material. Three-dimensional angiographic
volume rendered, curved reformatted and segmented images were generated on a
dedicated workstation. This report is based on interpretation of all of these
images.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Sequenced Acquisition 14.0 s, 14.0 cm; CTDIvol = 50.2 mGy (Head) DLP =
702.4 mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 5.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 59.9 mGy (Head) DLP =
30.0 mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 4.7 s, 37.4 cm; CTDIvol = 31.0 mGy (Head) DLP =
1,156.1 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Head) = 1,888 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  None.

FINDINGS: 

CT HEAD WITHOUT CONTRAST:
Parenchymal hemorrhage centered in the right occipital area measuring 6.6 x
3.1 cm (series 2:13) with intraventricular extension to the occipital horn of
the right lateral ventricle. There is mass effect with partial effacement of
the occipital horn and body of the right lateral ventricle.  Small amount of
overlying subarachnoid hemorrhage (series 2:13).  There is no evidence of
acute large territory infarct.

No calvarial fracture.  The paranasal sinuses are essentially clear.

CTA HEAD:
Mild atherosclerotic calcification of the internal carotid arteries.  The
vessels of the circle of ___ and their principal intracranial branches are
patent with no marked stenosis, occlusion, or aneurysm formation.  The dural
venous sinuses are patent.

CTA NECK:
Moderate calcific atherosclerotic changes of the aortic arch. The carotid and
vertebral arteries and their major branches are patent with no marked stenosis
or occlusion. There is no evidence of internal carotid stenosis by NASCET
criteria.

OTHER:
Subpleural scarring in the apices and in the anterior aspect of the right
upper lobe.  No suspicious pulmonary nodules or masses.  The visualized
portion of the thyroid gland is within normal limits. There is no
lymphadenopathy by CT size criteria.  Patulous appearance of the esophagus. 
The remainder the visualized aerodigestive tract is within expected limits.

IMPRESSION:


1. Right occipital temporal hemorrhage with intraventricular extension as
described above.
2. No underline vascular malformation.  No acute large territorial infarct.
3. No intracranial arterial aneurysm, occlusion or marked stenosis. 
Unremarkable CTA of the head.
4. No ICA stenosis by NASCET criteria.  Allowing for atherosclerotic
calcification, unremarkable CTA of the neck.
5. Additional findings as described above.

Brief Hospital Course:
Mrs. ___ presented to the colorectal service at ___ on 
___ for an open partial colectomy and hemorrhoidectomy. 
She tolerated the procedure well without complications (Please 
see operative note for further details). After a brief and 
uneventful stay in the PACU, the patient was transferred to the 
floor for further post-operative management.  
Neuro:  Pain was well controlled on dilaudid PCA, which was soon 
switched over to oral oxycodone, Tylenol once she tolerated oral 
intake.
CV: Vital signs were routinely monitored during the patient's 
length of stay. Her postop Hct was 29.1. Overnight on POD1, she 
had low urine output with SBP in the ___. She was given a 
500cc LR bolus x2. Her Hct was 25.6. We transfused her 2 units 
of pRBC and 10mg IV Lasix. Cardiology was consulted and from the 
echocardiography and history, they agreed that this might be due 
to hypovolemia and her home medications. They recommended 
stopping her home valsartan and diltiazem, and to keep her 
propafenone until her blood pressure improved. Her rhythm has 
been sinus the entire hospital course. On POD2, she was making 
adequate urine and was becoming hypertensive, and so we 
restarted her home valsartan. On POD3, she was given half of her 
home diltiazem per cardiology recommendations (30mg BID) and was 
progressively given 60mg BID later that afternoon. On POD4, her 
heart rate, although remaining in sinus rhythm, was in the ___ 
when we held her propafenone and diltiazem as she was NPO and so 
she was given IV metoprolol for rate control instead. On POD5, 
she was restarted on her propafenone and diltiazem after her NG 
tube was removed and was allowed sips of water with her 
medication. She experienced no other issues from thereon.  
Pulm:  The patient was encouraged to ambulate, sit and get out 
of bed, use the incentive spirometer, and had oxygen saturation 
levels monitored as indicated.
GI: The patient was initially kept NPO after the procedure. The 
patient was later advanced to clears. However, on POD4, she had 
nausea & vomiting. We inserted an NG tube that put out 200cc of 
nonbilious ouput. This was later removed on POD5 and she was 
given sips of clears, then clears, and later tolerated a regular 
diet at time of discharge. 
GU: Patient had a Foley catheter that was removed on POD2. Urine 
output was monitored as indicated. At time of discharge, the 
patient was voiding without difficulty.  
ID:  The patient's vital signs were monitored for signs of 
infection and fever. The patient was started on/continued on 
antibiotics as indicated.
Heme: The patient had blood levels checked post operatively 
during the hospital course to monitor for signs of bleeding. The 
patient had vital signs, including heart rate and blood 
pressure, monitored throughout the hospital stay.  
On ___, the patient was discharged to rehab. At discharge, 
she was tolerating a regular diet, passing flatus, stooling, 
voiding, and ambulating independently with a bit of assistance. 
She will follow-up in the clinic in ___ weeks. This information 
was communicated to the patient directly prior to discharge.

Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the hospital after a Low Anterior Resection 
for surgical management of Rectal Cancer. You have recovered 
from this procedure well and you are now ready to return home. 
Samples from your colon were taken and this tissue has been sent 
to the pathology department for analysis. You will receive these 
pathology results at your follow-up appointment. If there is an 
urgent need for the surgeon to contact you regarding these 
results they will contact you before this time. You have 
tolerated a regular diet, are passing gas and your pain is 
controlled with pain medications by mouth. You may return home 
to finish your recovery.  
 
Please monitor your bowel function closely. You may or may not 
have had a bowel movement prior to your discharge which is 
acceptable, however it is important that you have a bowel 
movement in the next ___ days. After anesthesia it is not 
uncommon for patients to have some decrease in bowel function 
but you should not have prolonged constipation. Some loose stool 
and passing of small amounts of dark, old appearing blood are 
expected. However, if you notice that you are passing bright red 
blood with bowel movements or having loose stool without 
improvement please call the office or go to the emergency room 
if the symptoms are severe. If you are taking narcotic pain 
medications there is a risk that you will have some 
constipation. Please take an over the counter stool softener 
such as Colace, and if the symptoms do not improve call the 
office. If you have any of the following symptoms please call 
the office for advice or go to the emergency room if severe: 
increasing abdominal distension, increasing abdominal pain, 
nausea, vomiting, inability to tolerate food or liquids, 
prolonged loose stool, or extended constipation.  
 
You have ___ laparoscopic surgical incisions on your abdomen 
which are closed with internal sutures and a skin glue called 
Dermabond. These are healing well however it is important that 
you monitor these areas for signs and symptoms of infection 
including: increasing redness of the incision lines, 
___ drainage, increased pain at the 
incision, increased warmth of the skin at the incision, or 
swelling of the area. Please call the office if you develop any 
of these symptoms or a fever. You may go to the emergency room 
if your symptoms are severe.  

You may shower; pat the incisions dry with a towel, do not rub. 
The small incisions may be left open to the air. If closed with 
steri-strips (little ___ adhesive strips) instead of 
Dermabond, these will fall off over time, please do not remove 
them. Please no baths or swimming for 6 weeks after surgery 
unless told otherwise by your surgical team.
 
You will be prescribed narcotic pain medication Oxycodone. This 
medication should be taken when you have pain and as needed as 
written on the bottle. This is not a standing medication. You 
should continue to take Tylenol for pain around the clock and 
you can also take Advil. Please do not take more than 3000mg of 
Tylenol in 24 hours. Do not drink alcohol while taking narcotic 
pain medication or Tylenol. Please do not drive a car while 
taking narcotic pain medication.
  
No heavy lifting greater than 6 lbs for until your first 
post-operative visit after surgery. Please no strenuous activity 
until this time unless instructed otherwise by Dr. ___ 
Dr. ___.  
 
Thank you for allowing us to participate in your care! Our hope 
is that you will have a quick return to your life and usual 
activities. 

Good luck!

EKG ___:

There is no evidence of acute hemorrhage, edema, mass effect, or

Single upright portable chest radiograph was obtained.  
Cardiomediastinal and hilar contours are normal. The lung 
volumes are slightly low. The previously seen right middle lobe 
opacity, better appreciated in the lateral view, today on this 
frontal view appears to have decreased since the prior study. No 
pleural effusion or pneumothorax is detected. If there is 
continued concern, frontal PA and lateral radiograph can be 
obtained when possible for further evaluation. 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
Ms. ___ is a ___ with a history of cerebral aneurism, HTN, 
HLD, who presented after day after being discharged for 
community acquired pneumonia after being found wandering the 
streets with altered mental status and a positive tox screen for 
opiates. 

ACTIVE ISSUES:
1.  ACUTE DELIRIUM WITH PARANOIA:  She presented with an acute 
state of delirium.  She was confused during her recent admission 
for CAP, though showed clearing of her mental status prior to 
discharge.  She was found wandering the streets at night, and 
while she was fully attentive and oriented (able to recount 
months/days of week backwards), she was paranoid in that she 
felt unsafe and targeted by "everyone around her."  Her tox 
screen was positive for opiates.  Her pharmacy confirmed that 
she had been prescribed a codeine-based cough syrup earlier in 
the month, which is likely the culprit.  A head CT ruled out 
intracranial pathology.  B12 and folate were sent and were 
normal.  A CXR showed interval improvement of her pneumonia and 
she had no fevers or white counts suggesting ongoing infection.  
Her mental status slowly improved.  Geriatrics was consulted, 
and she was given small doses of zyprexa to control anxiety.  At 
the time of discharge, she had regained insight into her 
delirium, admitting that she had concerns over the safety of her 
neighborhood for some time, and could not overcome her fears at 
the time of admission.  She was discharged to spend a few days 
with her brother and sister-in-law for closer monitoring.  

2. COMMUNITY ACQUIRED PNEUMONIA:  She was recently admitted with 
CAP and completed a 5 day course of levaquin during this 
hospitalization.  She showed great improvement both clinically 
and radiographically.

3. HOME SAFETY:  She was evaluated by social work for growing 
concerns over her ability to care for herself at home, which 
were voiced by her son.  She was resistant to a home safety 
evaluation, but she and her son were provided with the number of 
a local agency to establish this service as an outpatient if 
they desire.

4. MEMORY IMPAIRMENT:  her son voiced concerns over slowly 
progressive memory deficits.  They were provided with the number 
for neurocognitive testing to be established as an outpatient.

INACTIVE ISSUES:
5.  HTN: she was continued on HCTZ/lisinopril with good control
6.  RECENT ___:  she had a recent prerenal ___ from previous 
hospitalization, resolved at the time of admission
7.  HYPERLIPIDEMIA:  continued zocor
8.  SYSTOLIC DYSFUNCTION:  Remained euvolemic and asymptomatic
9.  ANEMIA: stable hematocrit, was started on iron supplements 
for suspected iron deficiency.

PENDING LABS AT DISCHARGE:  none
TRANSITIONAL CARE ISSUES:
-Will benefit from in-home safety evaluation and supplemental 
services to maximize and maintain independence.
-may benefit from neurocognitive testing

Discharge Instructions:
Dear ___,

___ were admitted to the hospital after being found confused on 
the street.  A urine test showed that ___ likely took a dose of 
a codeine-based cough syrup, which often causes confusion in 
elderly people.  Your recent pneumonia and poor sleep also 
probably contributed.  Your mental status cleared after spending 
a few days in the hospital, and ___ were then discharged.

Please avoid codeine in the future.  ___ are likely sensitive to 
strong pain medicine, such as narcotics, as well.

___ completed a course of antibiotics for pneumonia.  Please 
continue all other medications as prescribed by your other

INDICATION:  ___ with head injury  // bleed?

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous axial images images of the brain were obtained without
contrast. Coronal and sagittal as well as thin bone-algorithm reconstructed
images were obtained.

DOSE:  DLP:  191 mGy-cm
CTDI:  52 mGy

COMPARISON:  CT head ___

FINDINGS: 

Embolization coil within a posterior communicating artery aneurysm cause
significant streak artifact limiting evaluation. There is no evidence of acute
intracranial hemorrhage, edema, mass effect or acute large territorial
infarction. Prominence of the ventricles and sulci are similar to the prior
study suggesting age-related atrophy. Periventricular white matter
hypodensities are nonspecific but may reflect chronic microvascular ischemic
disease. The basal cisterns are patent. Gray-white matter differentiation is
preserved.

There is no fracture. Minimal opacification of left apical mastoid air cells
is similar to prior studies. The middle ear cavities are clear. The paranasal
sinuses are normally pneumatized. There are atherosclerotic calcifications of
the cavernous internal carotid arteries.  There is a large volume of cerumen
in the left external auditory canal.

IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of acute intracranial abnormality
2. Large volume of cerumen in the left external auditory canal.
 |
| RR | ['CT C-SPINE W/O CONTRAST'] | EXAMINATION:  Cervical spine CT

INDICATION:  ___ s/p fall  // fx?

TECHNIQUE:  Non contrast CT axial images of the cervical spine were obtained. 
Sagittal and coronal reconstructions were performed.

DOSE:  DLP: 1634 mGy-cm; CTDI:  73 mGy

COMPARISON:  MR cervical spine ___

FINDINGS: 

There is no evidence of acute fracture or malalignment. Cervical lordosis is
mildly exaggerated. Vertebral body heights are preserved. The dens is normally
positioned between the lateral masses of C1. The prevertebral and paraspinal
soft tissues are unremarkable.

There are multilevel multifactorial degenerative changes with anterior and
posterior osteophytes. Posterior disc osteophyte complexes cause mild to
moderate central canal narrowing from C3 through C7. Uncovertebral facet joint
hypertrophy results in central canal narrowing severe at C3-C4 on the left,
C4-C5 on the right, bilaterally at C5-C6 and C6-C7.

The thyroid is grossly normal. Included lung apices are clear.

IMPRESSION:


1. No evidence of acute fracture or malalignment.
2. Degenerative changes resulting in severe neural foraminal narrowing at
multiple levels.

| RR | ['CTA HEAD AND CTA NECK'] | EXAMINATION:  CTA HEAD AND CTA NECK Q16 CT NECK

INDICATION:  ___ year old woman with fall, left sided weakness and neglect//
assess for vascular lesion, bleed

TECHNIQUE:  Contiguous MDCT axial images were obtained through the brain
without contrast material. Subsequently, helically acquired rapid axial
imaging was performed from the aortic arch through the brain during the
infusion of Omnipaque intravenous contrast material. Three-dimensional
angiographic volume rendered, curved reformatted and segmented images were
generated on a dedicated workstation. This report is based on interpretation
of all of these images.

DOSE:  Acquisition sequence:
   1) Sequenced Acquisition 16.0 s, 16.0 cm; CTDIvol = 50.2 mGy (Head) DLP =
802.7 mGy-cm.
   2) Stationary Acquisition 5.5 s, 0.5 cm; CTDIvol = 89.8 mGy (Head) DLP =
44.9 mGy-cm.
   3) Spiral Acquisition 4.8 s, 37.7 cm; CTDIvol = 31.0 mGy (Head) DLP =
1,169.5 mGy-cm.
 Total DLP (Head) = 2,017 mGy-cm.

COMPARISON:  MR brain ___, CT head ___

FINDINGS: 

CT HEAD WITHOUT CONTRAST:
Hypodensity is noted within the right insula, corona radiata, and precentral
gyrus, worrisome for late acute/subacute infarction.  No additional areas of
possible infarction are identified.  There is no evidence of no evidence of
hemorrhage, edema, or mass.  The ventricles and sulci are prominent,
compatible with global cerebral atrophy.  Periventricular and subcortical
white matter hypodensities are nonspecific and likely reflect chronic small
vessel ischemic disease.

The visualized portion of the paranasal sinuses, mastoid air cells, and middle
ear cavities are clear. The visualized portion of the orbits are unremarkable.
Several small, ossified, dural-based right frontal and left parietal masses
likely represent meningiomas.  Calcifications are noted in the bilateral
cavernous carotid arteries.  Orthopedic hardware extends from the occiput to
the upper cervical spine, as visualized.

CTA HEAD:
Dense calcifications are noted within the bilateral cavernous and supraclinoid
internal carotid arteries, resulting in moderate to severe stenosis at these
levels.  The vessels of the circle of ___ and their principal intracranial
branches otherwise appear normal without occlusion or aneurysm formation.  The
dural venous sinuses are patent.

CTA NECK:
The V2 segment of the left vertebral artery is not well visualized for a
short-segment (3:124), reconstituting as it enters the foramen transversarium
at the level of C4 (3:131).  Otherwise, the remainder of the carotid and
vertebral arteries and their major branches appear normal with no evidence of
stenosis or occlusion. There is no evidence of internal carotid stenosis by
NASCET criteria.

OTHER:
The visualized portion of the lungs are clear.  The visualized portion of the
thyroid gland is within normal limits. There is no lymphadenopathy by CT size
criteria.

IMPRESSION:


1. Late acute/subacute infarctions involving the right insula, coronary
radiata, and precentral gyrus, confirmed on subsequent MRI examination.
2. No evidence for acute intracranial hemorrhage.
3. Focal, short segment non opacification of the V2 segment of the left
vertebral artery, suggestive of occlusion versus less likely chronic
dissection at this level.
4. Moderate to severe stenosis of the bilateral cavernous and supraclinoid
internal carotid arteries.  No evidence for focal occlusion or aneurysm
greater than 3 mm.

INDICATION:  ___ patient with left-sided weakness and confusion.

obtained.

COMPARISON  CTA head and neck ___, CT head ___

FINDINGS: 

There is slow diffusion within the right postcentral gyrus and right insula
with associated FLAIR hyperintensity, without associated hemorrhage in this
area.  There is susceptibility artifact within the left medial frontal lobe
without hyperdensity on prior CT, likely representing chronic blood products.

There is prominence of the ventricles and sulci secondary to age-related
involutional changes.  There are nonspecific periventricular and subcortical
white matter FLAIR hyperintensities, likely sequela of chronic small vessel
ischemic disease.  Again seen is a calcified 1.1 cm right frontal convexity
extra-axial mass with associated susceptibility artifact (11:21) likely
representing calcified meningioma as seen on prior CT.

The major visualized arterial vascular flow voids are preserved.  There is
mild mucosal thickening of the bilateral ethmoid air cells.  The remaining
paranasal sinuses appear clear.  Patient is status post bilateral cataract
surgery.  The bilateral mastoid air cells and the visualized soft tissues
appear unremarkable.  Susceptibility artifact associated with the surgical
hardware within the cervical spine and the occiput partially obscures
visualization of the adjacent structures.

IMPRESSION:


1. Late acute to early subacute infarctions within the right postcentral gyrus
and right insula, without associated hemorrhage.
2. Chronic blood products within the medial left frontal lobe.
3. Age-related involutional changes with nonspecific white matter signal
abnormality, likely a sequela of chronic small vessel ischemic disease.
4. Again seen is a 1.1 cm right frontal calcified meningioma.
5. Mild paranasal sinus disease.

___, M.D. on the telephone on ___ at 1:35 am, 2 minutes after discovery
of the findings.

Mrs. ___ is ___ with history of asthma and sarcoidosis with 
recent admission to OSH for sarcoidosis who presents for chest 
palpitations and shortness of breath.

On ___ at 8pm while she was sitting down watching a movie, 
Mrs. ___ felt a severe back pain deep in her upper back 
that was ___ in severity, constant, and felt like a heavy 
pressure. She took a Tylenol ___ mg, which usually improves her 
mild back pain which she has had for months, and it only 
temporarily provided relief until the pain came back a few 
minutes later. She attempted to go to sleep while continuing to 
have pain, and when she lay on her side, she was not able to 
breath. She fell asleep on her back a few hours after the pain 
initially began.

She awoke on 3am on ___ to urinate and noted continued back 
pain of the same quality. However, when she attempted to stand 
up off the toilet, she felt palpitations and shortness of 
breath. The palpitations felt like her tongue was vibrating in 
her throat and she had a flutter in the back of her neck, near 
where she had back pain. Her shortness of breath felt like she 
could not catch her breath.

She was not able to go to sleep after these symptoms started, 
and she noticed that the palpitations and SOB worsened when she 
tried to lay backwards or took a deep inhalation. The 
palpitation continued with time and her SOB worsened with time. 
She also began to feel a sharp, focal substernal chest pain that 
did not abate with time. She had episodes of SOB in the past due 
to her asthma, but had never had such severe back pain, 
palpitations, or chest pain. At 5am, she called her family and 
decided to go to the ___ ED.

# Pericarditis/pericardial effusion:
Patient had been diagnosed at ___ with pericardial 
effusion on ___ and treated with prednisone taper and 
colchicine. Symptoms had improved until the day before admission 
when she had acute onset SOB, palpitations and chest/back pain. 
She was hypotensive and tachycardic on admission, c/f tamponade. 
TTE showed an increase in pericardial effusion compared to 
prior, and possible constrictive physiology though no signs of 
tamponande. Her hemodynamic status improved with fluids after 
admission. Cardiology, and cardiac and thoracic surgery were 
consulted immediately. The fluid pocket was deemed too shallow 
for therapeutic or diagnostic pericardiocentesis. She was 
started first on high dose ibuprofen and switched to 
indomethacin and continued on colchicine and prednisone. Her 
symptoms improved markedly over the next 2 days. In light of her 
clinical improvement and the high risk of pericardiocentesis, 
pericardial fluid was not sampled. Several infectious serologies 
came back negative, including HIV, TB and Lyme. Pulm was 
consulted, and it was not thought to be sarcoid, as pericardial 
effusion in these cases are rare, and she had no arrhythmia. VZV 
was considered as the patient had a zoster rash and had recently 
completed Valtrex course before admission, but ID and 
dermatology were consulted did not think this was a likely 
etiology. Diagnosis at discharge was viral or idiopathic 
pericarditis and pericardial effusion. She was discharged with 
cardiology ___ TTE, and instructions for a 
prednisone and indomethacin taper, and to continue colchicine 
until cardiology ___. 

# Hypertension
The patient was hypotensive on admission, but was normotensive, 
then hypertensive for the remainder. She had two episodes of 
hypertension to 180s-190s without symptoms. Likely due to 
combination of underlying essential hypertension (possibly), 
high-dose NSAIDs, and mineralocorticoid effect of prednisone as 
well as anxiety from being in the hospital, which she endorsed. 
She was discharged on amlodipine 5mg. Need for this 
antihypertensive should be readdressed after completion of 
treatment regimen for pericarditis. 

# Herpes zoster rash
Patient had dried/crusted & healing rash on the L breast, flank 
and back in a dermatomal distribution. Had completed course of 
Valtrex before admission. Appeared most c/w zoster, Derm was 
consulted as some concern for sarcoid rash per Pulmonology recs. 
Dermatology agreed that rash was most likely due to Zoster and 
recommended no further evaluation and she was given Aquafor 
ointment.

What happened in the hospital?
- You came in because of chest/back pain, shortness of breath 
and palpitations. These symptoms were likely caused by increased 
fluid around your heart, also known as a pericardial effusion, 
that was diagnosed in the beginning of ___. We treated this

infectious tests came back negative. The cause was most likely a 
virus that led to inflammation of the heart's lining.

combined with steroids, for which high blood pressure is a

History of Present Illness:
Ms. ___ is a ___ female with history of Chiari 
malformation s/p several spinal procedures, chronic daily 
headache, and recent posterior fossa decompression and 
cranocervical fusion who presents with nausea and vomiting.

Patient was at baseline day prior to presentation in ED. Awoke 
on day of admission to ED with marked nausea and vomiting. 
Reports ___mesis that day with associated 
crampy abdominal pain and chills but no fevers (max measured 
temp ___. Baseline headache with no change in vision or new 
numbness or tingling. Addiitonally denies associated diarrhea 
with last BM at 7 am that day that was formed without melena or 
hematochezia. Unable to tolerate po intake throughout day. 
Denies recent travel, sick contacts. Works as ___ and 
___ on ___. Cannot recall last meal. Called 
covering PCP who suggested stomach flu and encouraged hydration. 
Presented to ED due to persistent emesis.  

Of note, patient with baseline daily headaches due to trigeminal 
neuralgia. These headaches are characterized by sharp pain, 
numbness of the left side of her face, occipital headache, 
occasional double vision, and jaw pain limiting po intake to 
soft foods. Per sister, this low po intake has caused her to 
lose a significant amount of weight recently. Headache usually 
associated with pain but has never been associated with this 
degree of vomiting. Takes fentanyl patch q72 hours, meperidine 
injection from PCP ___ weekly, and occasional po meperedine. She 
recently saw her PCP ___ ___ and was given Demerol 125 mg IM 
and Zofran 4 mg IM. Also of note, patient underwent posterior 
fossa decompression and cranocervical fusion for chiari 
malformation at the ___ in ___. 
Operation complicated by "stroke-like" symptoms per patient with 
onset of L arm weakness and R eye diplopia. Reports R eye 
diplopia resolved and L arm weakness still present but much 
improved. 

In the ED, initial vitals were: 99.7 75 131/71 18 99% RA. Labs 
were significant for WBC 9.1, H/H 14.6/41.2, plt 388, Na 139, K

was given Zofran IV 4mg x 2, Ativan 1mg IV x 7, Compazine 10mg 
IV x2, 4L NS, and 1L D5NS. CTV head with no evidence of dural 
venous sinus thrombosis and no acute process. CT abdomen/pelvis 
with no acute process. Vitals prior to transfer were: 98.5 112 
143/95 16 97% RA. 
 
On the floor, patient vomiting clear emesis during interview. 
Reports persistent nausea and crampy diffuse abdominal pain. 
Endorses baseline headache but no change in vision, new numbness 
or tingling sensation. Last sexual activity many years prior. 
Additionally denies chest pain, cough, shortness of breath, 
dysuria, rash, joint pain.

1. S/p suboccipital craniectomy and occipital cervical fusion to

of the brain parenchyma and vascular structures in the posterior 
fossa. 
2. Where not affected by streak artifact, there is no evidence 
for acute 
hemorrhage or parenchymal edema.  No hydrocephalus. 
3. Evaluation of the lower aspects of bilateral sigmoid sinuses 
is limited by streak artifact.  Other major dural venous sinuses 
appear patent. 

CT ABD & PELVIS WITH CONTRAST Study Date of ___ 4:54 AM 
No acute intra-abdominal process, no findings to explain 
patient's symptoms.  Appendix not visualized although no 
inflammatory changes identified in the right lower quadrant. 

 
Brief Hospital Course:
========
SUMMARY: 
========
___ female with history of Chiari malformation s/p 
several spinal procedures, chronic daily headache, and recent 
posterior fossa decompression and cranocervical fusion in ___ who presented with nausea and vomiting. 
=============
ACUTE ISSUES: 
=============
# Nausea and vomiting: Patient presented with acute onset of 
nausea and vomiting day prior to admission and was not 
tolerating po intake. Associated abdominal cramping but denied 
associated diarrhea. Given abrupt onset, mild temperature of 
___, and winter season, we attempted to assess for norovirus but 
patient was without bowel movements during her hospital stay. 
CTAP in ED showed no evidence of acute intra abdominal process 
to explain her symptoms and lipase and LFTs were normal. CTV 
performed in ED as well with no evidence of thrombus or acute 
process. Neurological exam on admission was non-focal further 
lowering suspicion for increased intracranial pressure. Patient 
denied recent sexual activity thus urine hcg was not acquired to 
assess for hyperemesis gravidarum or ectopic pregnancy given LLQ 
tenderness on admission exam. Lactate elevated in ED but 
resolved with IVF resuscitation. On day of discharge, patient 
with marked decrease in nausea, no emesis, and tolerating po 
medications and dietary intake well. Nausea and vomiting 
ultimately felt to be due to likely viral gastroenteritis. 

# Leukocytosis: Patient with leukocytosis to 15 on ___. This 
was attributed to likely viral gastroenteritis described above 
given absence of other localizing symptoms of infection.  UA 
checked but bland, culture was pending at time of discharge. 
Blood cultures from ED also no growth to date at time of 
discharge, but final read pending. 

# Concern for ICP: During ___ hospital stay noted to have 
slight drooping of right upper eyelid compared to left when eyes 
open on ___. Cranial nerve, EOMI, cerebellar testing, and 
strength testing intact/at baseline. Patient's clinical status 
with regards to nausea and vomiting were also overall improving 
at that time, so concern for increased ICP was lowered. 

# Hyponatremia: Patient was hyponatremic to 132 on day of 
discharge without overt clinical signs of hyponatremia. This was 
thought to be due to po repletion with water only by patient 
versus dehydration. PO intake was encouraged, particularly with 
salty foods. 

==============
CHRONIC ISSUES: 
==============
# Trigeminal neuralgia: Patient underwent posterior fossa 
decompression and cranocervical fusion for chiari malformation 
at the ___ in ___ in ___. Pain was managed 
with Fentanyl Patch 75 mcg/h TD Q72H and carisoprodol *NF* 700 
mg oral BID:PRN HA/muscle pain. Home po meperidine was held. 

# GERD: Patient with known small hiatal hernia noted on CTAP. 
Initially transitioned  oral PPI to IV due to inability to 
tolerate po medications. Changed back to oral PPI at time of 
discharge. 

# HDL: Continued home Atorvastatin 20 mg PO/NG DAILY 

# HTN: Continued home Atenolol 75 mg PO/NG DAILY

Transitional Issues: 
=====================

You came into the hospital due to nausea and vomiting. We 
performed a scan of your head and your abdomen and did not find 
anything concerning. We therefore think that your nausea and 
vomiting were likely due to a viral gastrointestinal illness. 
You improved with intravenous fluids and anti-nausea medications 
and were able to tolerate your oral medications and food on the 
day of discharge. 

You should follow up with your primary care physician after 
leaving the hospital. 

We wish you the best. It was a pleasure caring for you during 
your hospital stay.

Brief Hospital Course:
Patient is an ___ woman with a past medical history 
significant for atrial-fibrillation, previously on pradaxa, s/p 
right parieto-occipital IPH in ___, who presented from 
Rehab after family noted the patient to be acting more confused, 
agitated, and difficulty with language more so than her 
baseline. 

The following issues were managed for the patient: 

#Acute onset confusion, agitation, aphasia: 

-The patient received a non contrast Head CT which showed the 
stable findings of right IPH. No evidence of new acute 
intracranial findings. 
-CTA showed stenosis of the left ICA without complete occlusion, 
prompting the team to look for stroke in the left brain given 
the patient's aphasia. Due to the patient's pacemaker, repeat 
head CT was done looking for signs of ischemic infarct. The head 
ct however did not show any changes. 
-After initial workup, the patient's blood pressure was 
monitored and controlled to below 160mmHg. Her home medications 
were re-started. 
-Possibility of seizure was also considered during admission and 
EEG was obtained. No epileptiform activity seen, but right 
slowing with encephalopathy was noted. 
-As the patient was noted to have an infected urine, IV 
ceftriaxone was started for presumed UTI causing acute delirium. 
As the patient was treated with fluids and antibiotics, her 
mental status improved. 

#IPH: 

-Patient is following neurosurgery and scheduled for follow-up 
in the outpatient setting. 
-At this time, her anticoagulation is being held due to her 
bleed. However, it was discussed for the need to resume 
anticoagulation in ___ weeks due to her atrial-fibrillation and 
risk of embolic stroke. 
-This is an ongoing issue that will need to be followed-up. 
-Patient was maintained on Keppra for seizure prophylaxis. 

# Atrial-Fibrillation: 
-The patient was noted to be in atrial-fibrillation on telemetry 
monitoring with normal heart rate controlled on atenolol. 

-Patient to receive non contrast head CT on ___. If stable, patient can resume home dose of Pradaxa the 
following ___ ( ___. If the patient's 
outpatient providers would like to switch from pradaxa to an 
alternative anticoagulant, this would be appropriate from a 
neurological perspective.  
 
-Patient has an appointment with neurosurgeon Dr. ___ on 
___ at 2:00PM. Discuss duration of antiseizure 
prophylaxis with Keppra.

Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted for sudden worsening in function with 
confusion, agitation, and difficulty speaking. You underwent an 
extensive work-up including infectious work-up , and head CT/CTA 
looking for worsening head bleed or new stroke. Fortunately, 
head imaging did not reveal any worsening pathology. 
Urine testing revealed presence of an infection and you were 
treated with IV antibiotics. 
Your blood thinners were continued to be held. 
EEG was also done which tests for presence of seizures, but no 
seizure activity was noted on this test. 

Please take your other medications as prescribed.  
Please followup with Neurology and your primary care physician 
as listed below.  
If you experience any of the symptoms below, please seek 
emergency medical attention by calling Emergency Medical 
Services (dialing 911). In particular, since stroke can recur, 
please pay attention to the sudden onset and persistence of 
these symptoms:  
- Sudden partial or complete loss of vision  
- Sudden loss of the ability to speak words from your mouth  
- Sudden loss of the ability to understand others speaking to 
you  
- Sudden weakness of one side of the body  
- Sudden drooping of one side of the face  
- Sudden loss of sensation of one side of the body

___ with CKD (baseline Cr 3.4) history of scleroderma on

# Ankle pain:  On physical exam the majority of pain was 
elicited by touching the skin and not by moving the joint.  
Therefore this was felt to be unlikely to be a primary joint 
process and more of a skin process.  Cellulitis was considered 
and the patient initially received a regimen of Vanc and

history, and bilateral physical exam findings.  The petechia

most likely a reactive soft tissue erythema.  The rash was 
improving and antibiotics were stopped with continued

Dermatology clnic.   

# Acute on chronic kidney disease (baseline Cr 3.0-3.4).  The 
patient presented with Cr elevated to 4.0 and hyperkalemia 
without EKG changes. Her captopril was held and she was given 
IVF.  She did not have reactive sediment or proteinuria so this 
was felt unlikely to be a flare of her scleroderma renal crisis. 
 Her creatinine improved back to baseline and captopril was

potassium.  She should have labs checked at her primary care

hospitalization.

The left atrium is mildly dilated. Color-flow imaging of the

definitively identify any intracardiac shunt. Left ventricular 
wall thicknesses are normal. The left ventricular cavity size is

systolic function is normal (LVEF 60%). The right ventricular 
free wall thickness is normal. The right ventricular cavity is

aspiration or pneumonia.
 
Brief Hospital Course:
___ obese woman with hypertension, asthma, obstructive 
sleep apnea, history of sarcoidosis, pulmonary embolism who 
presented initially with four episodes of vomiting, then had 
usual chest pain with exertion

# Chest pain: Differential initially included unstable angina, 
PE, PNA, and musculoskeletal pain.  EKG showed no concerning 
changes.  Troponin neg x2.  TTE did not reveal any wall motion 
abnormalities, though study limited by body habitus.  EF 60%.  
Clinical picture inconsistent with PNA without cough, fever, 
leukocytosis.  Patient's chest pain was reproducible on exam.  
Therefore, it was most likely of musculoskeletal etiology.  The 
day after admission, she had no chest pain, and she felt like 
her usual self.  She was not tachypneic or hypoxic during this 
admission, therefore PE was considered unlikely.

# Bradycardia- Sinus bradycardia down to low ___.  Patient 
asymptomatic and hemodynamically stable.  Metoprolol decreased 
to 50mg daily.

# Vomiting: Unsure of the etiology but symptoms resolved prior 
to transfer to the floor.  Patient tolerated regularly meals 
during her hospitalization.  Most likely viral gastroenteritis. 
LFTs and lipase were normal.

# Chronic kidney disease: Creatinine at baseline

# Chronic right sided and diastolic heart failure- Continue home 
lasix 40 mg po qdaily

# Hypothyroidism: Continue home levothyroxine

# Sarcoidosis: Currently not on steroids

# Hypertension, benign: Continued home felodipine, decreased 
metoprolol due to bradycardia, lisinopril stopped due to high K+ 
on admission

# Asthma: Continue home medications

# Transitional issues- 
-follow up on blood pressure medication adjustment- metoprolol 
dose was decreased due to bradycardia, lisinopril stopped due to 
K+ of 5.2

- Text: INDICATION:  History of pulmonary embolism, now with pleuritic chest pain,
here to evaluate for pulmonary embolism.

COMPARISON:  CTA of the chest dated ___.  Chest radiographs performed
earlier the same day on ___.

TECHNIQUE:  Multidetector CT imaging of the chest was obtained using the CTA
protocol following the uneventful administration of 100 mL Omnipaque
intravenous contrast.  Sagittal and coronal reformations, as well as bilateral
oblique maximal intensity projections were generated and reviewed.

FINDINGS:  Suboptimal bolus timing limits evaluation of the subsegmental
pulmonary arteries due to poor opacification.  Within this limitation, there
is no filling defect in the central pulmonary arteries to suggest pulmonary
embolism.  The pulmonary arterial trunk is normal in caliber.  The heart is
top normal in size without pericardial effusion.  There is no evidence of
acute right heart strain.  The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber without
evidence of acute aortic syndrome.

The thyroid gland is incompletely visualized.  No significant mediastinal,
hilar or axillary lymphadenopathy is seen.  Small bilateral axillary lymph
nodes do not meet CT size criteria for lymphadenopathy.  The esophagus is
unremarkable.

The central tracheobronchial tree is patent.  Within the pulmonary parenchyma,
there are patchy opacities in the right middle lobe and lingula which likely
represent atelectasis.  No focal consolidation, pleural effusion or
pneumothorax is present.  No suspicious pulmonary nodules or masses are
identified.

This study is not tailored for evaluation of subdiaphragmatic contents. 
Within this limitation, the visualized upper abdomen is unremarkable.

BONES AND SOFT TISSUES:  No suspicious lytic or sclerotic lesions are
detected.  Multilevel degenerative changes are noted in the thoracic spine
with anterior osteophyte formation and loss of intervertebral disc space with
endplate sclerosis. There is kyphotic curvature of the thoracic spine.

IMPRESSION:
1.  Suboptimal bolus timing limits evaluation of the subsegmental levels of
the pulmonary arteries. Within that limitation, there is no central pulmonary
embolism. 
2.  Patchy opacities of the right middle lobe and lingula most likely
represent atelectasis.

- Note_Type: DS
- Text:  
Name:  ___                 Unit No:   ___
 
Admission Date:  ___              Discharge Date:   ___
 
Date of Birth:  ___             Sex:   F
 
Service: SURGERY
 
Allergies: 
No Known Allergies / Adverse Drug Reactions
 
Attending: ___
 
___ Complaint:
Rectovaginal fistula
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
gracilis flap mobilization, aborted RVF repair

 
History of Present Illness:
The patient is a ___ woman who had a
previous history of a gynecologic malignancy managed with
hysterectomy and low anterior resection with proximal
diverting ileostomy.  This procedure was complicated by a
rectovaginal fistula, which has been managed conservatively
for quite some time as the patient was under aggressive
chemotherapy.  She had evidence of disseminated disease and
once she had completed her chemotherapy and was considered to
be cancer free for a sufficient amount of time she agreed to
proceed with a perineal repair of her rectovaginal fistula
with gracilis flap.  She understands the risks, benefits of
the procedure and agrees to proceed
 
Past Medical History:
PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
*) h/o breast cancer, BRCA1, s/p b/l mastectomy, currently in 
remission
*) h/o thyroid nodule, on synthroid
*) h/o ___ fever" treated with course of abx

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
*) right sided lumpectomy
*) bilateral mastectomy with recontruction, ___
*) LSC oophorectomy, ___ 
*) thyroid nodule removal

OB/GYN: G1P0
She is menopausal since ___ yo. She has never had an episode of
vaginal bleeding since going through menopause. She denies a h/o
uterine fibroid. Her last pelvic U/S was done in ___, and to
her knowledge was normal. She does have a h/o abnormal paps, but
has not required biopsy. 

 
Social History:
___
Family History:
Denies h/o bleeding/clotting disorder. Extensive family h/o 
breast, ovarian and colon cancer.

 
Physical Exam:
General: Awake and alert in no apparent distress
Cardiac: Regular rate and rhythm
Pulm: Breathing comfortably on room air
GI: Soft, non-tender, non-distended
 
Pertinent Results:
___ 05:18AM BLOOD WBC-13.1* RBC-3.66* Hgb-11.6 Hct-33.9* 
MCV-93 MCH-31.7 MCHC-34.2 RDW-11.9 RDWSD-40.4 Plt ___

K-3.6 Cl-99 HCO3-30 AnGap-12

Brief Hospital Course:
The patient was admitted to the colorectal surgery service on 
___ and had a gracilis flap mobilization, perineal 
dissection, anorectal exam under anesthesia and biopsyof 
perineal mass.The patient tolerated the procedure well. Full 
records of the procedure can be found in the separately dictated 
operative report. The patient had previously planned to undergo 
a rectovaginal fistula repair, however in the operating room the 
patient was found to have a recurrent high grade malignancy and 
the procedure was aborted. She was informed of the decision to 
not proceed with the procedure and the findings in the operating 
room after she awoke from anesthesia.  Her primary doctor and 
oncologist were informed about what was found in the operating 
room.  A biopsy of the mass was taken and sent for pathology.
 .
 Neuro: Post-operatively, the patient received Morphine PCA with 
good effect and adequate pain control. When tolerating oral 
intake, the patient was transitioned to oral pain medications.
 .
 CV: The patient was stable from a cardiovascular standpoint; 
vital signs were routinely monitored.
 .
 Pulmonary: The patient was stable from a pulmonary standpoint; 
vital signs were routinely monitored.
 .
 GI/GU: Post-operatively, the patient was given IV fluids until 
tolerating oral intake. Her diet was advanced when appropriate, 
which was tolerated well. She was also started on a bowel 
regimen to encourage bowel movement. Foley was removed on POD#2. 
Intake and output were closely monitored.
 .
 ID: The patient's temperature was closely watched for signs of 
infection.
 .
 Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin during 
this stay, and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as 
possible.
 .
 At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile 
with stable vital signs, tolerating a regular diet, ambulating, 
voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. She 
will follow up with her oncologist regarding her diagnosis of 
recurrent disease.  
 
Discharge Medications:
1. Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q8H pain

3. Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO 3X/WEEK (___)

5. Venlafaxine 75 mg PO BID

RX *docusate sodium 100 mg 1 capsule(s) by mouth Twice daily 
Disp #*30 Capsule Refills:*0

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Ovarian cancer

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
* Please monitor your incision lines closely for signs of 
infection: opening of the incision, increased redness, increased 
pain, if you have a fever greater than 101, swelling of the 
tissues around the incision line, drainage of 
green/yellow/grey/white/thick drainage, increased pain at the 
incision line, or increased warmth.
 * You may shower with incisions. Let the warm water run over 
the incisions, pat all areas dry with a clean towel, and keep 
open to air but as clean and dry as possible. If the incisions 
become irritated, you may apply a dry sterile gauze dressing to 
the incision line. Please follow-up with Dr. ___ 
questions related to your most current surgery.
 * It is important that you continue to keep a pillow between 
your legs while you are sitting or lying in bed. This is 
allowing the blood flow to remain in these areas.
 * Do not sit or lay on sacral area.  Please change position 
while in bed or in a chair frequently. Please walk frequently. 
Please use a cushion under your bottom when sitting in a chair. 
Please avoid frequent bending at the waist or lifting anything 
greater than 5 pounds until cleared by Dr. ___.  Please 
continue good hygiene.

patient preference.

History is obtained by husband at bedside. Patient was recently diagnosed with UTI in the setting of fatigue and dysuria and started on Bactrim ___, the day of presentation to ___. She then developed diarrhea and vomiting, so her husband took the patient to ___. There, she had increasing difficulty with dyspnea. Labs were checked, with CO2 > 100. In this setting, she was noted to have an episode of unresponsiveness, with stiffening with fine tremors of the hands and feet, which resolved with Ativan (unknown dose). She was intubated by ___ ___ physicians shortly thereafter. Labs there notable for WBC 19.5, normal chemistry and lactate of 5.8. She was transferred to ___ for further management. On arrival to ___, she was febrile to 102.2, HRs up to 138 and BPs down to 88/60. VBG 7.14/71/54/26. Troponin 0.21 to 0.62 but CK-MB flat. LP with 2 WBC (80% lymph), protein 31, glucose 95 (serum 159). UA with 16 wbc, positive nitrites, negative ___, few bacteria. Serum tox and urine tox negative. She was given 3L IVFs (1 at OSh and 2 in our ___ with good BP response. She also received Vancomycin 1g, Cefepime 2g, Clindamycin 900mg, Keppra 1g IV. CT head unremarkable. CT torso also performed, which showed a 3 mm sub solid nodule and areas of ground-glass opacities with subtle ___ areas may represent pneumonia versus sequela of aspiration as well as innumerable hypodensities in the spleen and an anterior mediastinal soft tissue density. Past Medical History: Myasthenia ___ (follows with Dr. ___, never history of crisis, not on steroids but was previously frequent UTIs Social History: ___ Family History: Unknown Physical Exam: PHYSICAL EXAM ON ADMISSION: =========================== Vitals: T102.2 HR 135 BP 114/67 RR 26 99% Intubation Gen: ill appearing HEENT: atraumtic, sclera non-icteric CV: tachycardic, no murmurs Pulm: rhonchi bl Abd: soft, non-distended Ext: warm, mottled **sedation weaned for 10 min, likely still with paralytic in system MS: No eye opening to noxious stimuli, no commands CN: Pupils 2.5->2, eyes aligned, no BTT, no corneals, no cough, intact VORs, no clear facial droop Sensory/Motor: no w/d to deep noxious stimuli Reflexes: absent, toes muteHEENT: NC/AT Pulmonary: Breathing comfortably on RA; no increased WOB Cardiac: warm, well-perfused Abdomen: soft, ND Extremities: wwp, no C/C/E bilaterally Skin: no rashes or lesions noted. Deltoids 4+/5. Not fatigable after 40 arm pumps. -Sensory: intact to light touch throughout -Reflexes: deferred -Coordination: deferred -Gait: Deferred PHYSICAL EXAM ON DISCHARGE: =========================== General: HEENT: NC/AT Pulmonary: Breathing comfortably on RA; no increased WOB Cardiac: warm, well-perfused Abdomen: soft, ND Extremities: wwp, no C/C/E bilaterally Skin: no rashes or lesions noted. Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY: ================ ___ is a ___ old woman with a history of myasthenia ___ (not on immune suppression, no history of exacerbations), anorexia, recurrent UTI who presented to ___ with several days of fatigue and dysuria progressing to vomiting and diarrhea with rapid development of dyspnea s/p intubation for hypercarbic respiratory failure, admitted to the neuro ICU with UTI sepsis and seizure activity possible myasthenia ___ flare. ICU Course: ============ Patient hypotensive on admission to the ICU in the mid ___ after 4L of IVF, with initial VBG ___ and Lactate 4.0. Doing well on IVFs boluses until around 3:30am when she developed acute hypotension to 56/46. Switched to Vanc/Zosyn as patient has a history of MG. Bedside TTE showed wall motion abnormality in the left ventricle, concerning for cardiogenic process. Dobutamine, levophed started. CVL placed for pressors. Cardiology consulted, felt most consistent with cardiogenic shock. Floated swan, and fluids stopped after CVP was 8. Attempted to obtain records from outpatient neurologist but unable to reach as it was the weekend. Repeated ABGs showed improving metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. pneumonia.  Recommend further evaluation with dedicated chest x-ray.
3. Thoracic spine compression deformities, recommend thoracic spine
radiographs.
